posts.title: Whiteboard
posts.mobiledoc:
{"version":"0.3.1","atoms":[],"cards":[["markdown",{"cardName":"card-markdown","markdown":"Coding interviews are stressful. I have been on both sides of many interviews and have seen the process go many different ways. Being self taught I had just as much to learn for the interview process as I did for actually doing my job.

![tweet](https://drake-ghost.s3.amazonaws.com/2016/10/1-tsHwsy-_jG4acGFOSYJRVA.jpeg)

## Preparation

Google has a notoriously difficult hiring process. So if you are looking for a golden standard to prepare for its not a bad place to look (not that Facebook, Apple and a lot of other companies aren't just as hard. In my opinion, Google has the most advanced process, which even includes a coaching call. I don't know if this applies to everyone or those like myself who may be self taught or non-traditional developers, but participating was very helpful. My coach was an awesome guy who has written about his experience getting hired at Google^[[Google Would Never Hire a Person Like Me](http://www.huffingtonpost.com/anthony-mays/google-would-never-hire-a-person-like-me_b_7538164.html) by Anthony Mays]. The call started with him explaining his basic tips to success:

1. **Repeat the Question Aloud** (in your own words)
* **State and clarify your assumptions**
  * scalability? you should ask.
  * clarify the function signature (input, output)
  * disambiguate the results
* **Use an Example**
  * visualize your data structure on the whiteboard
  * gives you something visual to work with
  * if they provide one, use it
* **Brainstorm**
  * think of multiple solutions and their time and space complexity. That way, if you have a solution of O<sub>(n)</sub> vs O<sub>(log n)</sub>, you know you should go for the latter. Getting to this point in the process should take **~5 mins**
* **Write Code**
  * no pseudocode, it looks like weakness
  * write small, and leave space
* **Test Code**
  * seriously, always do it. If the interviewer has to point it out they will be disappointed
  * go line by line, on the whiteboard and act as compiler
* **Practice a Lot**

The last point is obviously the most important. You'll want to study algorithms^[A popular recommendation is [*Introduction to Algorithms*](https://www.amazon.com/Introduction-Algorithms-3rd-MIT-Press/dp/0262033844) by Thomas Cormen et al.] as much as you can, and dynamic programming. Brush up on your [discrete math](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Discrete_mathematics). Be familiar with as many data structures as possible.^[You can find plenty of great implementations of data structures [here](http://blog.benoitvallon.com/category/data-structures-in-javascript/)] It may be helpful to memorize a few algorithms and data structures and their space/[time](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Time_complexity) complexity^[Big O Notation can be tricky to understand at first but is actually [pretty simple](https://justin.abrah.ms/computer-science/how-to-calculate-big-o.html). When calculating space time complexity they really only care about the curve, so don't give something like "O<sub>n</sub> + 8". This [cheatsheet](http://bigocheatsheet.com/) is useful].


But mostly just find a good set of coding interview questions and just tackle them on a whiteboard.^[There are many great sources for interview questions. My coach recommended [*Cracking the Coding interview*](https://www.amazon.com/Cracking-Coding-Interview-Programming-Questions/dp/098478280X/ref=sr_1_3?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1481065319&sr=1-3&keywords=cracking+the+coding+interview) and I have found the email list of [Interview Cake](https://www.interviewcake.com/) provides some excellent questions for practice] A lot of the problems that you are given are underspecified. Part of the process is to ask the correct questions to fully understand the question. Show your work, it is as important as the "correct answer", arguably more so for the purposes of an interview. Keep in mind that generally, no one expects you to know every API exactly^[A lot of this advice comes directly from [Google employees](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oWbUtlUhwa8). I also recommend this [blog post](http://steve-yegge.blogspot.com/2008/03/get-that-job-at-google.html)], so be honest when you don't know something as long as it isn't something you should know.

## Simple Questions:
Every interview will start with some basic conversational questions that can generally be answered simply, like:

* What is the DOM?
* How does the [browser work](https://www.html5rocks.com/en/tutorials/internals/howbrowserswork/)?
* What is a closure?
* How does this work?
* Describe a few ways to communicate between a server and a client. Describe how a few network protocols work at a high level (IP, TCP, HTTP/S/2, UDP, RTC, DNS, etc.)
* What is the node.js [event loop](https://nodejs.org/en/docs/guides/event-loop-timers-and-nexttick/)
* difference between `==` & `===`
* `.apply` vs `.bind` vs `.call`
* pass-by-value vs pass-by-reference
* what is duck typing?
* what is IIFE? Why do it?

**Javascript Scope:**
If we execute this Javascript, what will the browser's console show?
```javascript
var text = 'outside';
function logIt(){
    console.log(text);
    var text = 'inside';
};
logIt();
```
**Answer:** The console will log undefined. To understand this, we need to explain a few things about Javascript.

* Function-level scope. Functions create new scopes in Javascript
* Declaration vs. assignment. A variable declaration simply tells the interpreter that a variable exists. By default it initializes the variable to undefined.
* Hoisting. In Javascript, variable declarations are "hoisted" to the top of the current scope. Variable assignments, however, are not.

There's not much of a way to prepare for this part besides just knowing your shit TBH.^[This article is really good for basic [frontend interview questions](https://performancejs.com/post/hde6d32/The-Best-List-of-Frontend-JavaScript-Interview-Questions-(written-by-a-Frontend-Engineer))]

# Whiteboard Questions
I've decided to include here a running log of interesting challenges that I stumble on in the hiring process and my responses to them at the time. I won't bore you with every dumb [fizz buzz](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fizz_buzz) question I see.

#### Palindrome
Given **two strings**, write a function that will determine **whether or not** the strings are **palindromes**.
<script src="https://gist.github.com/camwes/03eb1e585a0087c3cd91.js"></script>
#### Jack & Jill
Given an **array of names**, write a program that will **sort the array** such that Jack always comes first and Jill always comes second. **Jill should come first if there are not Jacks**.

<script src="https://gist.github.com/camwes/81ee8493a08a76881b5b.js"></script>


#### Object Equality
given **two inputs of any type**, determine if the inputs **are equal**. i.e. if they are strings or numbers they should be equal, if they are objects all of their **attributes should be equal**.
<script src="https://gist.github.com/camwes/5f9b31753b70ca8d2bbf.js"></script>

#### Simple Recursion
Given a **JSON object** that you know little about, how can you **return data of a specific type**? e.g. write a function that can return and array of all of the numbers/strings/etc in an object:
<script src="https://gist.github.com/camwes/7df44825cc3a66e71ff3.js"></script>

#### Stock Price Challenge
Suppose we could access yesterday's **stock prices as a list**, where: 

The **indices are the time in minutes past trade opening time**, which was 9:30am local time. The **values are the price in dollars** of Apple stock at that time. So if the stock cost $500 at 10:30am, `stock_prices_yesterday[60]`returns 500.

Write an efficient function that takes `stock_prices_yesterday` and **returns the best profit** I could have made from 1 purchase and 1 sale of 1 Apple stock yesterday.

First I made sure that I understood the structure of the data:
```javascript
var stock_prices_yesterday = [24, 2, 11, 4, 6, 3];
```
I asked some clarifying questions to get a better understanding of the problem and implemented a really terrible solution because I did not properly understand the problem. Once I fully understood the problem I described a brute force approach which I was encouraged to implement:
```javascript
function maximumProfit(prices) {
  var lowest = prices[0];
  var maximumProfit = 0;

  for (var i = 0; i < prices.length; i++) {      
    for (var j = i; j < prices.length; j++) {
      var profit = prices[j] - prices[i];
      if (profit > maximumProfit) {
        maximumProfit = profit;
      }
    }
  }
  console.log(maximumProfit);
}
maximumProfit(stock_prices_yesterday);  // Correctly returns 9
```
This solution works but I quickly pointed out that it was an **O<sub>(n<sup>2</sup>)</sub>** and we could surely do better. So I thought for a few minutes for a a better solution. Eventually, the interviewer just asked me to simply think about what I would have to do at each step to determine the maximum. Soon I came up with a much better solution:
<script src="https://gist.github.com/camwes/e5bd03950c8447e364cad61ce11bf069.js"></script>
What I came up here was a dynamic programming solution that I developed simply by thinking about what had to occur at each step in order to return the correct answer. What I came up satisfied my goal of a solution that could run in **O<sub>(n)</sub>** time **O<sub>(1)</sub>** space^[It's explained pretty clearly in this [stack overflow question](http://stackoverflow.com/a/7086577)]
#### Some Arithmetic
Given an **array of integers**, determine **whether or not** there exist two elements in the array **(at different positions)** whose sum is equal to some target value. Examples: `[5, 4, 2, 4], 8 --> true` `[5, 1, 2, 4], 8 --> false`

Make sure you know what you need to return, it can save you a lot of work. Boolean returns are the best because once true you can bail. With this problem you should be able to brainstorm to a few solutions:


* two loops: **O<sub>(n)</sub>** solution
* sort and add: **O<sub>(log n)</sub> solution**
* mystery solution: **O<sub>n</sub>** solution

> **Tip: Use space to optimize time**^[from the time-memory tradeoff [wikipedia article](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Space%E2%80%93time_tradeoff)]:
"Biological usage of time–memory tradeoffs can be seen in the earlier stages of animal behavior. Using stored knowledge or encoding stimuli reactions as "instincts" in the DNA avoids the need for "calculation" in time-critical situations. More specific to computers, look-up tables have been implemented since the very earliest operating systems."

My Answer:
<script src="https://gist.github.com/camwes/1cb0965785dc5c6cc5affdcf6be3be47.js"></script>

#### Set Data Structure/Random Number Generator
Implement a set data structure^[From the above link on [data structures](http://blog.benoitvallon.com/data-structures-in-javascript/the-set-data-structure/)]</sup> that supports Insert, Remove, and GetRandomElement efficiently. Example: If you insert the elements 1, 3, 6, 8 and remove 6, the structure should contain [1, 3, 8]. Now, GetRandom should return one of 1, 3 or 8 with equal probability.  
> Who needs a getRandom on a data structure? That should tell you what this question is about.

* Get: **O<sub>(1)</sub>**
* Set: **O<sub>(1)</sub>**
* Random **O<sub>(1)</sub>**

They can all be done in constant time, which is about the best you can do.^[We say about because you can actually do better than O<sub>(1)</sub> by using a [bitmap](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bit_array). In general, [bit manipulation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bit_manipulation) is an important concept to understand for optimizing space and time of algorithms when applicable.]
> Tip use multiple data structures


<script src="https://gist.github.com/camwes/8e03333495de8befad8f413c3e412413.js"></script>

### [In-Place Shuffle of Array](https://www.interviewcake.com/question/java/shuffle)
The challenge of shuffling is a great example of when one might take a [naive approach](https://blog.codinghorror.com/the-danger-of-naivete/) where a more elegant solution is required. Usually, a naïve algorithm is just oversimplified and inefficient, but in this case it is wrong. The most well-known approach to shuffle is the [Fisher-Yates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fisher%E2%80%93Yates_shuffle) AKA [Knuth](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Donald_Knuth) shuffle. I would read Mike Bostok's [beautiful explainer](https://bost.ocks.org/mike/shuffle/).
<script src="https://gist.github.com/camwes/efd8055ea37a112de7b731280d36c7ce.js"></script>

### [Merge Sorted Arrays](https://www.interviewcake.com/question/java/merge-sorted-arrays)
<script src="https://gist.github.com/camwes/70cf9b77a78edc0937681271b9ed5c26.js"></script>

### [Kth to Last Node in a Singly Linked List](https://www.interviewcake.com/question/javascript/kth-to-last-node-in-singly-linked-list?utm_source=weekly_email&utm_source=drip&utm_campaign=weekly_email&utm_campaign=Interview%20Cake%20Weekly%20Problem%20%23249:%20Kth%20to%20Last%20Node%20in%20a%20Singly-Linked%20List&utm_medium=email&utm_medium=email&__s=jksqj7avviyhkzszgkrj)
<script src="https://gist.github.com/camwes/13ebbdae7f7ddd9299e8b1e67f219237.js"></script>

### [Bracket Validator](https://www.interviewcake.com/question/java/bracket-validator)
<script src="https://gist.github.com/camwes/e999dc91b5dba4d87eeddf0b239016a2.js"></script>

### Find Contraindications
#### Naive solution
The brute force solution in this instance is very straightforward:
```javascript
function getContraindicationNaive(medications, contraindications) {
  let contraindication = false;
  contraindications.forEach(([a,b]) => {
    if (medications.includes(a) && medications.includes(b)) {
      contraindication = true;
    }
  })
  return contraindication;
}
```
Using the `includes` method makes this code look very succinct but it is important to remember that the worst case for the method is **O<sub>(n)</sub>**, making the complexity here quadratic, we can certainly do better. The solution to these types of questions almost always includes using a hashmap.
<script src="https://gist.github.com/camwes/cb2e4c85036136de986ed66a7ef34792.js"></script>

## Reading Library
What follows is a list of books that I have/am/plan reading. These books typically came recommended to me and I think they represent a good subset of the large amount of literature out there.
#### Software Engineering
* Code - Charles Petzold | [amazon](https://www.amazon.com/Code-Language-Computer-Developer-Practices-ebook/dp/B00JDMPOK2/ref=sr_1_1?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1504997158&sr=1-1&keywords=code+charles+petzold)
* The Pragmatic Programmer - Andrew Hunt and David Thomas | [amazon](https://www.amazon.com/Pragmatic-Programmer-Journeyman-Master/dp/020161622X)
* The Art of Comuter Programming - Donald Knuth | [wiki](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Art_of_Computer_Programming) | [amazon](https://www.amazon.com/Computer-Programming-Volumes-1-4A-Boxed/dp/0321751043)
* Introduction to Algorithms 3rd Edition by Thomas H. Cormen | [amazon](https://www.amazon.com/Introduction-Algorithms-3rd-MIT-Press/dp/0262033844/ref=sr_1_1?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1504997205&sr=1-1&keywords=algorithms+third+edition)
* The Algorithm Design Manual - Steven S. Skiena | [amazon](https://www.amazon.com/Algorithm-Design-Manual-Steven-Skiena-ebook/dp/B00B8139Z8/ref=mt_kindle?_encoding=UTF8&me=)
* Algorithms - Jeff Erickson
* Software Architecture Patterns - Mark Richards
* Cracking the Coding Interview - Gayle Laakmann McDowell | [amazon](https://www.amazon.com/Cracking-Coding-Interview-Programming-Questions/dp/0984782850/ref=pd_lpo_sbs_14_img_0?_encoding=UTF8&psc=1&refRID=EF6RYXKKWEMRM2TNG242)
* Programming Pearls - Jon Bentley | [amazon](https://www.amazon.com/Programming-Pearls-2nd-Jon-Bentley/dp/0201657880/ref=pd_sim_14_2?_encoding=UTF8&psc=1&refRID=EF6RYXKKWEMRM2TNG242)

#### Web Development
* High Performance Websites - Steve Souders | [amazon](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0028N4WHY/ref=dp-kindle-redirect?_encoding=UTF8&btkr=1)
* Javascript: The Good Parts - Douglas Crockford | [amazon](https://www.amazon.com/JavaScript-Good-Parts-Douglas-Crockford/dp/0596517742)
* Secrets of the Javascript Ninja - John Resig | [amazon](https://www.amazon.com/Secrets-JavaScript-Ninja-John-Resig/dp/193398869X)
* CSS The Definitive Guide | [amazon](https://www.amazon.com/CSS-Definitive-Guide-Visual-Presentation/dp/1449393195/ref=sr_1_1?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1505006427&sr=1-1&keywords=css+the+definitive+guide)
* CSS Pocket Reference | [amazon](https://www.amazon.com/CSS-Pocket-Reference-Visual-Presentation/dp/1449399037/ref=sr_1_1?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1505006627&sr=1-1&keywords=css+pocket+reference)
* CSS Cookbook 3rd Edition | [amazon](https://www.amazon.com/CSS-Cookbook-3rd-Animal-Guide/dp/059615593X)
* CSS3 for Web Developers

posts.html:
Coding interviews are stressful. I have been on both sides of many interviews and have seen the process go many different ways. Being self taught I had just as much to learn for the interview process as I did for actually doing my job.

Preparation

Google has a notoriously difficult hiring process. So if you are looking for a golden standard to prepare for its not a bad place to look (not that Facebook, Apple and a lot of other companies aren't just as hard. In my opinion, Google has the most advanced process, which even includes a coaching call. I don't know if this applies to everyone or those like myself who may be self taught or non-traditional developers, but participating was very helpful. My coach was an awesome guy who has written about his experience getting hired at Google[1]. The call started with him explaining his basic tips to success:

  1. Repeat the Question Aloud (in your own words)
  • State and clarify your assumptions
    • scalability? you should ask.
    • clarify the function signature (input, output)
    • disambiguate the results
  • Use an Example
    • visualize your data structure on the whiteboard
    • gives you something visual to work with
    • if they provide one, use it
  • Brainstorm
    • think of multiple solutions and their time and space complexity. That way, if you have a solution of O(n) vs O(log n), you know you should go for the latter. Getting to this point in the process should take ~5 mins
  • Write Code
    • no pseudocode, it looks like weakness
    • write small, and leave space
  • Test Code
    • seriously, always do it. If the interviewer has to point it out they will be disappointed
    • go line by line, on the whiteboard and act as compiler
  • Practice a Lot

The last point is obviously the most important. You'll want to study algorithms[2] as much as you can, and dynamic programming. Brush up on your discrete math. Be familiar with as many data structures as possible.[3] It may be helpful to memorize a few algorithms and data structures and their space/time complexity[4].

But mostly just find a good set of coding interview questions and just tackle them on a whiteboard.[5] A lot of the problems that you are given are underspecified. Part of the process is to ask the correct questions to fully understand the question. Show your work, it is as important as the "correct answer", arguably more so for the purposes of an interview. Keep in mind that generally, no one expects you to know every API exactly[6], so be honest when you don't know something as long as it isn't something you should know.

Simple Questions:

Every interview will start with some basic conversational questions that can generally be answered simply, like:

  • What is the DOM?
  • How does the browser work?
  • What is a closure?
  • How does this work?
  • Describe a few ways to communicate between a server and a client. Describe how a few network protocols work at a high level (IP, TCP, HTTP/S/2, UDP, RTC, DNS, etc.)
  • What is the node.js event loop
  • difference between == & ===
  • .apply vs .bind vs .call
  • pass-by-value vs pass-by-reference
  • what is duck typing?
  • what is IIFE? Why do it?

Javascript Scope:
If we execute this Javascript, what will the browser's console show?

var text = 'outside';
function logIt(){
    console.log(text);
    var text = 'inside';
};
logIt();

Answer: The console will log undefined. To understand this, we need to explain a few things about Javascript.

  • Function-level scope. Functions create new scopes in Javascript
  • Declaration vs. assignment. A variable declaration simply tells the interpreter that a variable exists. By default it initializes the variable to undefined.
  • Hoisting. In Javascript, variable declarations are "hoisted" to the top of the current scope. Variable assignments, however, are not.

There's not much of a way to prepare for this part besides just knowing your shit TBH.[7]

Whiteboard Questions

I've decided to include here a running log of interesting challenges that I stumble on in the hiring process and my responses to them at the time. I won't bore you with every dumb fizz buzz question I see.

Palindrome

Given two strings, write a function that will determine whether or not the strings are palindromes.

Jack & Jill

Given an array of names, write a program that will sort the array such that Jack always comes first and Jill always comes second. Jill should come first if there are not Jacks.

Object Equality

given two inputs of any type, determine if the inputs are equal. i.e. if they are strings or numbers they should be equal, if they are objects all of their attributes should be equal.

Simple Recursion

Given a JSON object that you know little about, how can you return data of a specific type? e.g. write a function that can return and array of all of the numbers/strings/etc in an object:

Stock Price Challenge

Suppose we could access yesterday's stock prices as a list, where:

The indices are the time in minutes past trade opening time, which was 9:30am local time. The values are the price in dollars of Apple stock at that time. So if the stock cost $500 at 10:30am, stock_prices_yesterday[60]returns 500.

Write an efficient function that takes stock_prices_yesterday and returns the best profit I could have made from 1 purchase and 1 sale of 1 Apple stock yesterday.

First I made sure that I understood the structure of the data:

var stock_prices_yesterday = [24, 2, 11, 4, 6, 3];

I asked some clarifying questions to get a better understanding of the problem and implemented a really terrible solution because I did not properly understand the problem. Once I fully understood the problem I described a brute force approach which I was encouraged to implement:

function maximumProfit(prices) {
  var lowest = prices[0];
  var maximumProfit = 0;

  for (var i = 0; i < prices.length; i++) {      
    for (var j = i; j < prices.length; j++) {
      var profit = prices[j] - prices[i];
      if (profit > maximumProfit) {
        maximumProfit = profit;
      }
    }
  }
  console.log(maximumProfit);
}
maximumProfit(stock_prices_yesterday);  // Correctly returns 9

This solution works but I quickly pointed out that it was an O(n2) and we could surely do better. So I thought for a few minutes for a a better solution. Eventually, the interviewer just asked me to simply think about what I would have to do at each step to determine the maximum. Soon I came up with a much better solution:

What I came up here was a dynamic programming solution that I developed simply by thinking about what had to occur at each step in order to return the correct answer. What I came up satisfied my goal of a solution that could run in O(n) time O(1) space[8]

Some Arithmetic

Given an array of integers, determine whether or not there exist two elements in the array (at different positions) whose sum is equal to some target value. Examples: [5, 4, 2, 4], 8 --> true [5, 1, 2, 4], 8 --> false

Make sure you know what you need to return, it can save you a lot of work. Boolean returns are the best because once true you can bail. With this problem you should be able to brainstorm to a few solutions:

  • two loops: O(n) solution
  • sort and add: O(log n) solution
  • mystery solution: On solution

Tip: Use space to optimize time[9]:
"Biological usage of time–memory tradeoffs can be seen in the earlier stages of animal behavior. Using stored knowledge or encoding stimuli reactions as "instincts" in the DNA avoids the need for "calculation" in time-critical situations. More specific to computers, look-up tables have been implemented since the very earliest operating systems."

My Answer:

Set Data Structure/Random Number Generator

Implement a set data structure[10] that supports Insert, Remove, and GetRandomElement efficiently. Example: If you insert the elements 1, 3, 6, 8 and remove 6, the structure should contain [1, 3, 8]. Now, GetRandom should return one of 1, 3 or 8 with equal probability.

Who needs a getRandom on a data structure? That should tell you what this question is about.

  • Get: O(1)
  • Set: O(1)
  • Random O(1)

They can all be done in constant time, which is about the best you can do.[11]

Tip use multiple data structures

In-Place Shuffle of Array

The challenge of shuffling is a great example of when one might take a naive approach where a more elegant solution is required. Usually, a naïve algorithm is just oversimplified and inefficient, but in this case it is wrong. The most well-known approach to shuffle is the Fisher-Yates AKA Knuth shuffle. I would read Mike Bostok's beautiful explainer.

Merge Sorted Arrays

Kth to Last Node in a Singly Linked List

Bracket Validator

Find Contraindications

Naive solution

The brute force solution in this instance is very straightforward:

function getContraindicationNaive(medications, contraindications) {
  let contraindication = false;
  contraindications.forEach(([a,b]) => {
    if (medications.includes(a) && medications.includes(b)) {
      contraindication = true;
    }
  })
  return contraindication;
}

Using the includes method makes this code look very succinct but it is important to remember that the worst case for the method is O(n), making the complexity here quadratic, we can certainly do better. The solution to these types of questions almost always includes using a hashmap.

Reading Library

What follows is a list of books that I have/am/plan reading. These books typically came recommended to me and I think they represent a good subset of the large amount of literature out there.

Software Engineering

  • Code - Charles Petzold | amazon
  • The Pragmatic Programmer - Andrew Hunt and David Thomas | amazon
  • The Art of Comuter Programming - Donald Knuth | wiki | amazon
  • Introduction to Algorithms 3rd Edition by Thomas H. Cormen | amazon
  • The Algorithm Design Manual - Steven S. Skiena | amazon
  • Algorithms - Jeff Erickson
  • Software Architecture Patterns - Mark Richards
  • Cracking the Coding Interview - Gayle Laakmann McDowell | amazon
  • Programming Pearls - Jon Bentley | amazon

Web Development

  • High Performance Websites - Steve Souders | amazon
  • Javascript: The Good Parts - Douglas Crockford | amazon
  • Secrets of the Javascript Ninja - John Resig | amazon
  • CSS The Definitive Guide | amazon
  • CSS Pocket Reference | amazon
  • CSS Cookbook 3rd Edition | amazon
  • CSS3 for Web Developers

Footnotes

  1. Google Would Never Hire a Person Like Me by Anthony Mays ↩︎

  2. A popular recommendation is Introduction to Algorithms by Thomas Cormen et al. ↩︎

  3. You can find plenty of great implementations of data structures here ↩︎

  4. Big O Notation can be tricky to understand at first but is actually pretty simple. When calculating space time complexity they really only care about the curve, so don't give something like "On + 8". This cheatsheet is useful ↩︎

  5. There are many great sources for interview questions. My coach recommended Cracking the Coding interview and I have found the email list of Interview Cake provides some excellent questions for practice ↩︎

  6. A lot of this advice comes directly from Google employees. I also recommend this blog post ↩︎

  7. This article is really good for basic frontend interview questions ↩︎

  8. It's explained pretty clearly in this stack overflow question ↩︎

  9. from the time-memory tradeoff wikipedia article ↩︎

  10. From the above link on data structures ↩︎

  11. We say about because you can actually do better than O(1) by using a bitmap. In general, bit manipulation is an important concept to understand for optimizing space and time of algorithms when applicable. ↩︎

   [https://www.amazon.com/Programming-Pearls-2nd-Jon-Bentley/dp/0201657880/ref=pd_sim_14_2?_encoding=UTF8&psc=1&refRID=EF6RYXKKWEMRM2TNG242]

Web Development
 * High Performance Websites - Steve Souders | amazon
   [https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0028N4WHY/ref=dp-kindle-redirect?_encoding=UTF8&btkr=1]
 * Javascript: The Good Parts - Douglas Crockford | amazon
   [https://www.amazon.com/JavaScript-Good-Parts-Douglas-Crockford/dp/0596517742]
 * Secrets of the Javascript Ninja - John Resig | amazon
   [https://www.amazon.com/Secrets-JavaScript-Ninja-John-Resig/dp/193398869X]
 * CSS The Definitive Guide | amazon
   [https://www.amazon.com/CSS-Definitive-Guide-Visual-Presentation/dp/1449393195/ref=sr_1_1?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1505006427&sr=1-1&keywords=css+the+definitive+guide]
 * CSS Pocket Reference | amazon
   [https://www.amazon.com/CSS-Pocket-Reference-Visual-Presentation/dp/1449399037/ref=sr_1_1?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1505006627&sr=1-1&keywords=css+pocket+reference]
 * CSS Cookbook 3rd Edition | amazon
   [https://www.amazon.com/CSS-Cookbook-3rd-Animal-Guide/dp/059615593X]
 * CSS3 for Web Developers

Footnotes

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 1.  Google Would Never Hire a Person Like Me
     [http://www.huffingtonpost.com/anthony-mays/google-would-never-hire-a-person-like-me_b_7538164.html] 
     by Anthony Mays ↩︎
     
     
 2.  A popular recommendation is Introduction to Algorithms
     [https://www.amazon.com/Introduction-Algorithms-3rd-MIT-Press/dp/0262033844] 
     by Thomas Cormen et al. ↩︎
     
     
 3.  You can find plenty of great implementations of data structures here
     [http://blog.benoitvallon.com/category/data-structures-in-javascript/] ↩︎
     
     
 4.  Big O Notation can be tricky to understand at first but is actually pretty
     simple
     [https://justin.abrah.ms/computer-science/how-to-calculate-big-o.html].
     When calculating space time complexity they really only care about the
     curve, so don't give something like "On + 8". This cheatsheet
     [http://bigocheatsheet.com/] is useful ↩︎
     
     
 5.  There are many great sources for interview questions. My coach recommended 
     Cracking the Coding interview
     [https://www.amazon.com/Cracking-Coding-Interview-Programming-Questions/dp/098478280X/ref=sr_1_3?s=books&ie=UTF8&qid=1481065319&sr=1-3&keywords=cracking+the+coding+interview] 
     and I have found the email list of Interview Cake
     [https://www.interviewcake.com/] provides some excellent questions for
     practice ↩︎
     
     
 6.  A lot of this advice comes directly from Google employees
     [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oWbUtlUhwa8]. I also recommend this blog
     post [http://steve-yegge.blogspot.com/2008/03/get-that-job-at-google.html] 
     ↩︎
     
     
 7.  This article is really good for basic frontend interview questions
     [https://performancejs.com/post/hde6d32/The-Best-List-of-Frontend-JavaScript-Interview-Questions-(written-by-a-Frontend-Engineer)] 
      ↩︎
     
     
 8.  It's explained pretty clearly in this stack overflow question
     [http://stackoverflow.com/a/7086577] ↩︎
     
     
 9.  from the time-memory tradeoff wikipedia article
     [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Space%E2%80%93time_tradeoff] ↩︎
     
     
 10. From the above link on data structures
     [http://blog.benoitvallon.com/data-structures-in-javascript/the-set-data-structure/] 
      ↩︎
     
     
 11. We say about because you can actually do better than O(1) by using a bitmap
     [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bit_array]. In general, bit manipulation
     [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bit_manipulation] is an important concept to
     understand for optimizing space and time of algorithms when applicable. ↩︎

{"version":"0.3.1","markups":[],"atoms":[],"cards":[["markdown",{"cardName":"card-markdown","markdown":"TLDR; you are invited to 505 Grove st EVERY Thursday evening for drinking, chilling and stimulating conversation.

I know a lot of dope people. If you are reading this, you are probably one of them. The best thing about knowing so many cool people is getting them together in the same room and observing the outcome. In an effort to create as many of these situations as possible we (Dan, Marcus and Cameron) are hosting a Thursday night salon, for the intermingling of minds, the imbibing of booze and the listening of music. You are encouraged to bring something to share (a vinyl to play, a bottle to drink, a friend, etc.) or just yourself. We only ask that you limit distractions, which means if your phone is out for longer than a host (or guest) deems necessary it could be confiscated.

The inspiration, if you are unfamiliar are the great salons of the French literary and artistic renaissance. From Wikipedia: "A salon is a gathering of people under the roof of an inspiring host, held partly to amuse one another and partly to refine the taste and increase the knowledge of the participants through conversation. These gatherings often consciously followed Horace's definition of the aims of poetry, "either to please or to educate" ("aut delectare aut prodesse est")."
"}]],"sections":[[10,0]],"ghostVersion":"3.0"}

posts.plaintext:
TLDR; you are invited to 505 Grove st EVERY Thursday evening for drinking,
chilling and stimulating conversation.

I know a lot of dope people. If you are reading this, you are probably one of
them. The best thing about knowing so many cool people is getting them together
in the same room and observing the outcome. In an effort to create as many of
these situations as possible we (Dan, Marcus and Cameron) are hosting a Thursday
night salon, for the intermingling of minds, the imbibing of booze and the
listening of music. You are encouraged to bring something to share (a vinyl to
play, a bottle to drink, a friend, etc.) or just yourself. We only ask that you
limit distractions, which means if your phone is out for longer than a host (or
guest) deems necessary it could be confiscated.

The inspiration, if you are unfamiliar are the great salons of the French
literary and artistic renaissance. From Wikipedia: "A salon is a gathering of
people under the roof of an inspiring host, held partly to amuse one another and
partly to refine the taste and increase the knowledge of the participants
through conversation. These gatherings often consciously followed Horace's
definition of the aims of poetry, "either to please or to educate" ("aut
delectare aut prodesse est")."

A few years ago I wrote [this blog post](__GHOST_URL__/blog/devops/) detailing my strategy for deploying node.js apps to Amazon EC2 instances. Since then I have gotten far more acquainted with the many services that AWS comprises, and have made great improvements to my development process as a result. Launching a scalable node.js app is something that can literally be done in less than 30 minutes these days with the correct workflow.

# Development
These days my development process has been greatly streamlined by not relying on programs like Grunt and Ruby Gems. I'm not shitting on these things, they are just a pain to support. I have an unofficial rule that if I can't `npm install` it, I ain't using it. I use Babel and Webpack in projects and it is a Godsend for dependency management. In addition to loading every file type that I need to handle, my webpack config does all of the following things:

- hot reloading to watch files
- running jshint and analysis on my javascript
- compressing all of my images
- minifying, concating and cache-busting my code
- gzipping my static assets and uploading them to S3

Assuming that you use webpack or your favorite build tool to do something similar that means that you've basically gotten the static assets part of your app figured out. When it comes to getting your server code in production though you probably get a little nauseous thinking about insuring that all of your dependencies are installed correctly and servers all that crap. This is where Docker makes your life a lot easier. Create a Dockerfile for every repo, even if all it does is npm install and expose the port. Containerizing your app makes it so much easier to deploy. This is especially true if you have several services your app depends on. The majority of my node apps use both Redis and MongoDB, so Docker makes life a lot easier.

The purpose of this guide isn't really to teach you how to use docker, but it is probably still helpful to run through some basics. 
## Docker Basics
**Build & Run Container**
```bash
docker build -t 'application:latest' -f Dockerfile .
docker run application
```

**Listing Running Containers**
```bash
docker ps
```
**Accessing a Container**
There is a `docker attach` command, but that is probably not what you want
```bash
docker exec -it <mycontainer> bash
```
**Listing Images**
```bash
docker images
```
**Tagging and Pushing Images**
```bash
docker tag docker_application:latest user/repo:latest
docker push user/repo:latest
```
#### Cleaning up Space
```bash
# list orphaned
docker volume ls -qf dangling=true
# clean orphaned
docker volume rm $(docker volume ls -qf dangling=true) 
# Delete all containers
docker rm $(docker ps -a -q)
# Delete all images
docker rmi $(docker images -q)
```
#### Docker References
* Docker compose yml [reference](http://54.71.194.30:4017/compose/yml/)
* Efficient Dockerfiles with [build cache](http://bitjudo.com/blog/2014/03/13/building-efficient-dockerfiles-node-dot-js/)
* Cleaning up volumes [tutorial](https://lebkowski.name/docker-volumes/)
* remove [untagged containers](http://jimhoskins.com/2013/07/27/remove-untagged-docker-images.html)
* Resources
  * Docker/MongoDB/Express [tutorial](https://medium.com/@sunnykay/docker-development-workflow-node-express-mongo-4bb3b1f7eb1e#.7ntjv9lzb)
  * Docker/Node/Redis [tutorial](https://github.com/msanand/docker-workflow)
  * Docker/MongoDB guide [from Docker](https://docs.docker.com/engine/examples/mongodb/)

## Production
All that remains is getting you server code running. But don't worry... this is the easy part. Amazon Web Services has become a behemoth, and a major reason Amazon's stock has soared in recent years. It is certainly a one stop shop. Simply:

1. Purchase a domain from [Route 53](https://aws.amazon.com/route53/?nc2=h_m1)
- Create an [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/?nc2=h_m1) Bucket for your static assets
- Create a [Cloudfront](https://aws.amazon.com/cloudfront/?nc2=h_m1) distribution and point to that bucket
- Get a TLS certificate issued via [Certificate Manager](https://aws.amazon.com/certificate-manager/?nc2=h_m1)
- Create a new Docker repo in [EC2 Container Registry](https://aws.amazon.com/ecr/)
- Launch a [VPC](https://aws.amazon.com/vpc/)
- Create an [ElastiCache](https://aws.amazon.com/elasticache/?nc2=h_m1) in VPC
- Start an [ElasticBeanstalk Environment](https://aws.amazon.com/elasticbeanstalk/?nc2=h_m1) in VPC
- Create a [CodePipeline](https://aws.amazon.com/codepipeline/?nc2=h_m1)
- Add your github repo as the source.
- Add build and test stages with [CodeBuild](https://aws.amazon.com/codebuild/?nc2=h_m1)
- Add a deploy step for ElasticBeanstalk specifying your production branch
- Point DNS record to ElasticBeanstalk URL

## Security
This [article](https://simplesecurity.sensedeep.com/web-developer-security-checklist-f2e4f43c9c56) is a very great, comprehensive overview of web developer security best practices.
#### [ECS CLI Tutorial](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/ECS_CLI_tutorial.html#ECS_CLI_tutorial_compose_service)
#### [ECS Compose](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/cmd-ecs-cli-compose.html)


Configure the command line clients
```bash
aws configure
ecs-cli configure --region us-east-1 --access-key ACCESS_KEY --secret-key SECRET_KEY --cluster cluster-name
```
You can start up some instances via the CLI
```bash
ecs-cli up --keypair id_rsa --capability-iam --size 2 --instance-type t2.medium
```
You have the option of creating services based on docker-compose files
```bash
ecs-cli compose --file docker-compose.yml create
```I set the services that I need up in ECS, and then work out the connections between services by [associating the address](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/reference/ec2/associate-address.html)
```bash
# 1. create an elastic ip
aws ec2 allocate-address --domain vpc
aws ec2 describe-addresses

# 2. Find the instance running a container
aws ec2 describe-instances

# 3. Associate elastic ip with instance
aws ec2 associate-address --allocation-id <AllocationId> --instance-id <InstanceId>

# 4. Add attribute to instance
aws ecs put-attributes --attributes name=elasticip,value=<ElasticIp>,targetId=<arn>
```
Now when creating a service that needs to connect, add a [task placement constraint](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/task-placement-constraints.html) for the instance with the elastic ip attribute and you can insure that the specific service is tied to an elastic ip address.




https://docs.mongodb.com/manual/reference/mongo-shell/
https://docs.mongodb.com/manual/reference/log-messages/#log-severity-levels

https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/compute/centralized-container-logs-with-amazon-ecs-and-amazon-cloudwatch-logs/

http://stackoverflow.com/questions/43033559/aws-codebuild-getauthorizationtoken-failed

https://jira.mongodb.org/browse/SERVER-13352

http://stackoverflow.com/questions/37861118/deploy-image-from-ec2-container-registry-to-elastic-beanstalk-credentials
"}]],"sections":[[10,0]],"ghostVersion":"3.0"}

Like most children our age we created trouble on a regular basis. I'll never forget one day when I was around the age of 5 or 6 my brother Brandon (who was a year older) and I did something particularly mischievous that elicited a response from Johnnie Mae that we had never seen. Usually filled with rage at the moment she realizes that we've broken something, or we had otherwise irked her nerves, this time she exhibited a calm that struck fear in our hearts. She called us into her room and tools is to sit down. Considering she normally forbade us from entering her room this only made us more terrified. She pointed to a wardrobe in the corner of her room.

"Inside of that wardrobe is where Betsy lives. When I was a little girl and I did something bad my mother would typically ask me to go fetch her a switch from a tree to get a speaking with. One day, I was housing around in the kitchen and knocked all of the plates in the house to the ground broke them into a million pieces. My mother was so mad at this she didn't send for a switch this time. You see my mother has some Native American blood in her and so she got a lot of old clothes and belongings passed down for generations. One such belonging was this old belt that her mother had made. The belt was beautiful h thick hand stitched buffalo hide that was beautiful to wear, but served another more sinister purpose. You see getting a whopping with this belt wasn't like getting hit with a normal belt. It had holes precisely drilled along the middle to cut down on wind resistance. The stitching was old and frayed to the point that you could cut yourself at parts. And midst importantly it has lashes at both ends. When my mother brought that belt out I knew it was gonna hurt, but I could never be ready for the pain I would feel when Betsy finally touched my backside. ## December 9, 1994 Johnnie Mae
* Fell sick one day before school
* Not allowed to visit her in the hospital
* Suffered cardiac arrest and went into a coma
* Came home from school and was asked to sit on her bed by Aunt D.D.
* Knew immediately she had passed
* Will never forget driving down the street with my dad and Ryan spotting Chellye on the street.

## Unknown Date: Robbins, IL
For the second time in the last few days I have been overcome with the desire to hear gospel music. I thumbed through my collection to see what music was at my perusal only to realize that I probably haven't added a new Gospel song to my library in years. Fortunately Gospel music is eternal (no pun intended) and the message and chord progressions don't change much over time. I started thinking about how pathetic R&B music has become and I started to wonder if Gospel would have suffered a similar devastation of talent by the ever-popular "autotune". I have become sidetracked. My original purpose in opening in my laptop was to start writing. I don't think a shred of an idea of what I will write but I am convinced that my new method of just writing the first thing that comes to my mind. It's quite ironic that I am trying this for the first time right now because I constantly recommend it to students that I assist with essays. I like to read fluid thought. I always request that they not edit anything that they send me, and to try their best not to preemptively come up with something to write. The mind is an incredibly capable tool that when let loose on paper can produce something much more complex and impressive than what our conscious minds can produce. I feel that sometimes the best thing to do is to take your mind out of the equation, put on some inspirational music, close your eyes and let your fingers directly translate whatever comes into your mind only as quickly as you can only to be interrupted by the occasional wine break. You may want to open your eyes though during the wine break because I dropped half a glass of red wine in my lap earlier and will smell like wine all day now... nice.

I want to discuss my grandmother. Not Ruth, but Johnnie Mae. If anyone asks me I will always say that her death was one of the most defining moments in my life, yet I don't talk much about the woman herself. I think the reason that I avoid the subject of my grandmother is that focusing on her shines a light on one of my biggest shames. I don't really remember the woman. I know that it was her heroic actions that are responsible for me being where I am today. Actions that were heroic in the most precise definition of the word: by saving my three brothers and I my grandmother ultimately sacrificed herself, a debt that I consider unpaid even today.

My earliest memories are from living in Robbins, IL. I can remember flashes of things earlier than my childhood in Robbins but these images are just: brief flashes of seemingly random moments that have no coherence or significance that I can understand. It is for this reason that I consider my memory to start with one consistent and motion picture memory. I am in the enormous backyard of our home. This is my grandmother's home and it is a beautiful one. The front yard was immense with a short fence at the head. I don't remember the fence being particularly aesthetically pleasing but we were the only one of our neighbors that had one and I considered this an accomplishment. There was not much of a porch but that did not prevent my grandmother from sitting out on those three steps and doing whatever it was that grandma did. From my memory it was three stories high with a basement that was accessible to the left as soon as you entered the front door. The basement was where my brothers and I were allowed to play with our toys, naturally making it our favorite room. It was often the room that had the washer and dryer in it, and naturally my brothers and I would take time getting in the dryer, but we had no clue how to turn it on so I assume today that some precautions were taken to keep us from figuring it out. My youngest brother Ryan could not play with us because he was only 1 at the time and Brandon and I were notorious for playing pretty rough. One of my mother's favorite stories back then and even today when she left the two of us together when I was one year old and Brandon was 2 and he put me in a shoebox and sat on me and told her that I was gone... I never found humor in this story. Brandon would actually talk to Ryan the same way that he talked to me. This I never understood because no matter how simply I spoke to Ryan all I ever got was incoherent gurgling and squeaking. I already thought Ryan was a strange looking baby. He had irregularly large eyes and a lot of curly jet-black hair like a baby Jimi Hendrix, not that I had any clue who Jimi was at the time. Brandon spoke to him though and I just stood there and waited for him to be done most of the time.

My room was incredible. It was on the third floor with long windows that allowed so much sun into the room during the day that I was under the impression that it was so high up that it was closer to the sun. This thought entertained me at night when I thought about opening the window one morning and blocking the sun from everyone else. Ma and grandma never left the windows open when they were not in the room with me because they were well aware of my adventurousness. Back in these days Brandon and I did absolutely nothing. We didn't play any sports, we didn't care to read anything, we didn't like carnivals or circuses. Well technically I did like circuses but one time a donkey at a small circus stepped on Brandon's toe and he refused to ever go back to anything that was even close to a circus. What we spent our time doing was largely just using our imagination and getting into trouble. Our playground to us the most magnificent place on earth: the backyard. First of this entire backyard was enormous in proportion to the rest of the house. But it wasn't just the space that made this place incredible. Sitting in the backyard was also an enormous boat that was not in use at the time and belonged to our Uncle Joe who owned the cleaners near our home. I remember my first visit to his dingy little cleaners mainly because he had a gigantic fish tank. After seeing it for the first time I was determined to get a pet to accompany Brandon on our adventures in the backyard. I must have been a persuasive kid because not too soon after the visit we had a new dog that we affectionately named Poochie. So back to the backyard, we have a gigantic boat and a broken down Cadillac. There are bushes to the left that sprout some little berry that grandma convinces us is poisonous. Not only do we not eat the berries, we don't even get near them.

Brandon and I played a wide assortment of games, most of which were pioneered by us right there on the spot. While I truly wish that I remember enough of the details of these doubtlessly complex and entertaining games most of that escapes me. What I do know is that we loved hide and seek. In this backyard it became a particularly tough game for an assortment of reasons. First, the entire backyard was game for hiding (except of course the berry bushes because you would die). Second, neither Brandon or I had the attention span to search for very long, so if you hid somewhere really good chances are the seeker would quit without telling you and just walk away, leaving you to hide for an undetermined amount of time in whatever uncomfortable place you are. You may not be able to read the contempt in my voice through these pages but that often happened to me. One day in particular I was hiding in some bushes near the house and there was faucet nearby. This was a particularly hot summer day and many mosquitoes were attracted to this area. I realized that it was a bad place to be in for a long time as soon as I got there but thought that finding me would be easy and so I would not be there long. You see with Brandon and my version of hide and seek the goal was not to really hide forever but to be caught by trying your best to scare the other person shitless when they came nearby. So when Brandon was in the area I got excited that he would get close enough for me to scare him but instead he wandered into the woods nearer the house. I decided that I could bare it for a little bit longer and sat there. I have no idea how long I was there but when I left the bush I was absolutely covered in mosquito bites. I was itching so badly I had no choice but to see grandma. She was much less than pleased at my state and rapped me a few times upside the head for it. I kept complaining that Brandon didn't look for me and made me stay there and she only would reply with "Stop being a tattletale" or "Brandon's not covered in mosquito bites is he? Did he put a gun to your head and make you do it?". These kinds of things annoyed me to the utmost and I never wanted to be around grandma when she was like that. She clearly favored Brandon above me and I could not determine why. Wanting my ma I asked her 'where is mama?' She replied that she was out with my father and this phrase struck me as very odd. I understood the concept of what a father but was not at all aware that I had one. Apparently I had lived with my dad the first few years of my life before my parents divorced right before Ryan was born. Naturally, I was curious to know more about this man and proceeded with a million questions for my grandmother. It was not long before she seemed frustrated, the first indication that I ever had of the animosity between my grandma and father, this man that I had never actually met.

I obsessed over the memories flash images in my head searching for his face in any of these fuzzy memories. There was one man that I vaguely remember. This man was really tall, strong, with a baldhead and an earring. I never could put my finger on exactly where this man came from in my memories but I had an undeniable feeling that this man was my father. I committed that image to memory so that the next time I saw this man I would not make the mistake of missing him. I was obsessed with the possibility that I would see this man soon and I told Brandon. He immediately began to brag that he knew our father. He knew what he looked like and how his voice sounded and more details. I could not wait to finally meet this man, but it would not happen that night and in fact would not happen for a long time. And the image that I had been holding on to I soon realized was not my father at all, but that of Michael Jordan. I was incredibly disappointed in this discovery. I didn't understand why I had never met this man and so it was easy for me to let go as a kid.
## April 8, 2006

[Harvey Illinois](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harvey,_Illinois)
I was born in Chicago Illinois in 1988. Early in my life my family moved around a lot between different neighborhoods on Chicago's south side, west side and south suburbs (namely Robbins, Harvey and Midlothian). Subsequently, I've known some pretty rough neighborhoods. This only changed when between 1994-1997 I lived with my grandmother in a small relatively safe neighborhood in the Roseland area until she passed away just before the Christmas of '97. As a result I moved from the south side to live with my paternal grandmother in Harvey. Having lived there briefly years before I was well aware of the dangers that lurked in the city: plagued with all of the same crimes as the city but with a fraction of the police force, there was a general sense that the drug dealers and gangsters were the ones really in charge in this town of just over 25 thousand residents. The local high school was


* Mayor Eric Kellogg named ["Top White Collar Criminal"](http://www.truecrimereport.com/2010/11/top_5_white_collar_villains.php)
* Former City Spokesman calls suburb ["dangerous"](http://articles.chicagotribune.com/2014-05-27/news/ct-harvey-aide-quits-met-20140526_1_sandra-alvarado-mayor-eric-kellogg-suburb)
* Justice Department [Investigation into HPD](http://www.justice.gov/crt/about/spl/documents/harvey_findings_1-18-12.pdf)
* [City of Harvey: Be ashamed](https://mrmiller23.wordpress.com/2013/04/23/city-of-harvey-be-ashamed/)
* [Warzone in Harvey](http://southsuburbanwire.blogspot.com/2013/02/warzone-in-harvey.html)
* [Alvarado sues city](http://www.chicagotribune.com/news/local/breaking/ct-harvey-discrimination-lawsuit-met-20150410-story.html)
* [Lawless Harvey](http://www.chicagotribune.com/news/watchdog/ct-harvey-residents-frustrated-met-20141225-story.html#page=1) 
#### Misuse of funds

* Kellogg paid off election fines with [strip club and gas station money](http://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-politics/7/71/350585/strip-club-frozen-food-gas-stations-linked-harvey-mayors-bailout)
* Kellogg settled the city's [outstanding debt](http://www.chicagotribune.com/suburbs/ct-harvey-chicago-water-met-20150120-story.html) to the city of Chicago but it is unclear how the city will afford the more than $200k/monthly bill while operating at a deficit.
* The city of Harvey is paying $1.4 million to settle a lawsuit accusing Mayor Eric Kellogg and a police detective of [sending a man to prison](http://www.prweb.com/releases/2010/03/prweb3726954.htm) because they wrongly thought he stole cocaine from them.

#### Corruption
![Willie Lloyd III](https://drake-ghost.s3.amazonaws.com/2015/03/37907_1341311540927_673013_n.jpg)

* Five relatives are on the city’s payroll – including a $170,000 position to his niece as city attorney. He, himself, was double dipping as mayor and School Superintendent earning him an additional $202,000 for the latter.
* In 2006, Mayor Kellogg ordered a police detective to take a gun seized from a convicted drug felon and make it “vanish” from evidence (Daily Southtown).
* The son of a former school board president who was convicted of fraud for stealing $10,000 in student aid money from the State of Illinois – was rehired by Mayor Kellogg and the City of Harvard. (Chicago Tribune).
* A Harvey mayoral candidate running against Mayor Kellogg was falsely arrested for political gains.
* The Illinois Press Association put the City of Harvey third on their list of “worst offenders in the state when it comes to the Illinois Open Meetings Act and the Illinois Freedom of Information Act” (Daily Southtown)
* Mayor Kellogg and the City of Harvey for paying Mustapha Farrakhan, son of Nation of Islam leader Louis Farrakhan, for part-time police work. The young Farrakhan has a police badge, drives an unmarked Harvey Police squad car, and is registered with the state to carry a gun as an official City of Harvey policeman. Yet Mustapha Farrakhan hasn’t worked [a single shift in four years](http://www.theblaze.com/stories/2013/04/22/why-is-farrakhans-cop-son-driving-a-taxpayer-funded-police-car-and-carrying-a-gun-when-he-hasnt-worked-a-shift-in-4-years/).

## April 1, 2009
As I make my way through the terminal at O'hare airport I am relieved that my hangover from the previous night has finally subsided just in time for my trans-Atlantic flight. Believe it or not, this hangover is a badge of honor, a relic of my first legal night of drinking. Back home in Chicago for the first time in over a year I had been afraid that I would have spent the night alone. With Stanford on the quarter schedule my spring break didn't coincide with that of most of my friends from high school. So, I texted around to find a drinking mate for the occasion and was lucky enough to get a response from an old crush who I had run cross country with in high school and who went to school in the city. We hadn't been in touch much since but were both eager to catch up. We wound up at a super divy karaoke bar on the north side called the Blue Frog where the bartender took extra amusement in denying serving me until the clock struck midnight. 
        When the time finally came she served me an obnoxiously alcoholic special off the menu which lethally combined about 5 different liquors in a way so fruity and delicious that a seven year old would love it for about 5 mins before blacking out. I only handled this drink slightly better than a seven year old would, and less than an hour later had downed four and was doing some serious pelvic thrusting while singing "Bump & Grind" on the stage. My female accomplice was apparently impressed enough with my moves that we were soon making out in between gulping our viciously alcoholic drinks, but to my dismay that's as far as I could swing it that night. Reflecting back on my exploits as I boarded the plane I resolved to finish what I had started with her last night the next time I was in Chicago, assuming its not another few years before we saw each other again. 
        Once I've taken my seat on the plane and the flight attendants start making announcements in English and French my excitement for my destination begins to escalate. In less than eight hours I'll be touching down in my home for the next three or so months, the City of Lights. Studying abroad in Paris had been eagerly awaiting since I started college, and although I unfortunately had to scrap my plans to study abroad for  the winter quarter, nothing could have prevented me from spending my spring in Paris. A few rows up I notice a classmate of mine undoubtedly heading to the same place: at 6'6" Jeff is unmissable in a crowd, and we'd gotten to know  each other pretty well from classes that we had taken together in the Stanford drama department. I start to wonder to myself how many other Stanford students might be on this flight, we're on a 757 jet and its a packed flight so I can only see a small portion of the people on the plane. The seat next to me is still unoccupied and I wonder if I could be lucky enough to be the only person to get a section to myself. Im in the row to the left of the plane with only two seats per row.
        Predictably, someone gets to the row and stops to sit down. I can't exactly put my finger on her age, but she can't be older than me. Long curly brown hair hangs out of the heather grey hood that she has drawn over her head. She's wearing an outfit that is obviously comfortable for the long flight, but nonetheless stylish: her blue skirt matches well with the tights that she's wearing which have this unique large V pattern that seems to be intended to direct my eyes down her legs to the grey 2 inch heels she's got on.  In one of the most understated ways, this young lady is drop dead gorgeious,and she takes her seat without saying anything. I glance over at her quickly, but she just looks ahead. So as not to be seen staring at her out of her peripheral vision I just get back to reading my book and listening to Amos Lee in my headphones. I assume this girl is french, mainly because of her style and figure my chances of wooing a french girl before I even land on the continent to be pretty bad. 
        After 15 minutes of waiting to taxi the attendant gets on the speaker to inform us that the runways are very backed up and it can be as long as an hour before we're able to take off. A predictable groan reverberates throughout the plane. My seat mate simply sighs and continues flipping through a magazine. After a few minutes I hear her strike up a conversation with a couple across the aisle in French so good that it initially confirms my suspicions that she is returning home. It's only once I start eavesdropping on their conversation (understanding as much as I can with my limited french and headphones over one of my ears) that I am surprised to find out that she is actually from Los Angeles. I get intrigued and take the headphone off completely to better hear. Soon she explains to them that she is on her way to Paris to study abroad and it finally occurs to me that she has to be in my program (since as I noted earlier most school's are on a different schedule). At first this is startling to me, because Stanford students study abroad in their junior year which would mean that she was in my class and I had never met or even seen her before. This seems unlikely after further consideration and I decide that she must be a senior or sophomore. I soon overhear her mention Stanford and inside my heart leaps at the confirmation. Prior to this I had carefully studied the photos of the girls in the program looking for the cutest and had not noticed this one. Perhaps, she was a late addition or just took a very terrible passport photo. I almost start to pull out my packet and determine which it is, but realize that I currently have the advantage that she doesn't seem to know that I'm in her program and decide that I will use this to my advantage.
        A few minutes pass and her conversation across the aisle ends. I wait a bit and then gently start with an "excusez moi, but I couldn't help overhearing..." Soon we're conversing and she's largely repeating to me in English what she had told the couple across the aisle. Like most Stanford students she is reluctant to say where she goes to school and only says that she's from Los Angeles but goes to a school in "Northern California" (the moment after people find out that you go to Stanford is always slightly embarrassing and awkward because they always assume that you're far smarter than you feel). I'm amused that she has no idea that I go to Stanford as well and decide to play with this for as long as I can. I am coy and tell her that I was born in Chicago and that my birthday was last night. I interrogate her about herself for a few minutes mixing in some random topics before she asks more questions about myself. And eventually she asks my name and I tell her. For some reason, I say my full name (probably because I'm in love with my name) and notice her pause. It's at this point that I realize that I may have given myself away. Despite the fact that I've never so much as seen this girl in my life Stanford is a small community and there is a decent chance that she has heard my name before. In fact, there's an even greater chance that she has been emailed several times by me because I had been class president the year before. Knowing that the jig is up I confess to her that I am actually in the same program that she's in. She smiles. Luckily she's entertained by my antics and immediately warms up. I ask her name and she replies Yasmine. I repeat it back to her and ask for some clarification, "is it Yaz-min or Yaz-meen?" She looks surprised by the question. "It is yaz-meen, but people can rarely pronounce it correctly so I don't mind either". I go on a short diatribe about how everyone deserves to have their name pronounced correctly... I share stories about bizarre things written on my cup at Starbucks. She seems entertained.
        Meanwhile we're still stuck on this runway (it's been over 45 minutes already) and we have little to entertain ourselves but talking to one another. We're covering a wide range of topics none of which I remember because I'm actually slightly hypnotized by the largest, most beautiful kiwi green eyes that I've ever seen. Her pupils are strangely dilated but it still can't take away from the amazing color and depth in her eyes. I try not to look as enthralled as I am, but I get the feeling that she doesn't notice my obsession with her eyes. She's very cordial and all the while I'm trying to figure out how in the world this cutie is in my class and I've never laid eyes on her before. We start comparing the circles that we run in and then it all starts to make sense. She generally runs in quiet, studious and largely asian crowds while my crowd is more of the loud, partying largely greek crowd. We express our excitement for the program. She explains to me that she is Algerian, which is why her French is so impeccable. She has family in Paris, and is excited that her aunt is going to pick her up from the airport when she arrives. I joke that I'll keep her around so that the French don't spit in the food of the guy with the horrendous American accent.
        After what seems like an eternity the jet is finally ready to take off. With that our conversation slows. We exchange a few words over the course of the flight about all kinds of subjects like what music we like or what dorms we've lived in, but both of us eventually get self-conscious about potentially over extending the conversation in the event that the other wants to sleep or read or something and at some point I close my eyes and before I know it I am actually asleep. It would not surprise me to awake to find the seat next to me empty, this mysterious, sexy girl only a figment of my imagination or character from a dream. It seems absurd to me but I know that somewhere deep in those green eyes of hers I had fallen in love.

Yasmine's Perspective
As I squeezed through the aisles scanning the numbers for my seat, lugging my packed suitcase along in haste, I spotted you from a few rows back. I recognized you as the guy Jeff was talking to in the boarding line outside. I didn’t know Jeff personally, but knew unmistakably and immediately that he too was from Stanford, having spotted him wandering campus on several occasions in my first three years on the farm. He had been on my previous flight from Los Angeles to Chicago, and now here he was, boarding for Paris. So I knew, too, that you must be from Stanford, and in our study abroad program. I heard you both talking about the Paris program, catching some words here and there as I sat in a seat eating a bagel and pretending to listen to music.

I approached our row and noticed that I’d be sitting next to you. I smiled furtively, enjoying the secret pleasure of knowing just a little bit more than you about the lay of the land. I hoisted my bag into the overhead compartment, climbed into my seat, and said hi, perhaps first, perhaps in response. In your t-shirt, jeans and boat shoes, you had no idea who I was. I was just some girl, and I basked in the mysteriousness of my presence. I quickly plugged in my little iPhone headphones-- nothing compared to the large stereo phones strung around your neck-- and settled into what I expected to be 8 hours of anticipation. But I was quickly interrupted by an older woman, sitting across the aisle from us, who complimented my St.Tropezienne sandals. We began to chat about Paris, she was leading a group of high school students from the midwest on a study abroad program, and she teased out of me that I too would be studying abroad in Paris, through Stanford. Alas, the jig was now up, and you and I suddenly knew the same amount about one another. Assuming of course, that you had overheard my conversation with our row neighbor, just as I had eavesdropped on your chat with Jeff.

And you had. You turned to me and asked if I was also in the Paris program, confirming that you too would be studying there. I feigned surprise. But for those next few hours we spent sitting on the runway in Chicago, for the first time in my life, I was surprised. I was intrigued, I was curious, I was impressed. We spoke about your birthday the day before, your scholarship donor who had an apartment in Paris, the fact that you were our president the year before and I had recognized your name from all those “giving tree” emails. And I kept thinking to myself, over and over, I want to be friends with him. We didn’t talk too much more as the flight took off, both of us trying to pass time watching movies, listening to music, sleeping.

And then we landed in Paris. We went our separate ways after picking up our bags, but not before we were both laughed at by French customs officers for having unnecessary student visas on our American passports. My aunt picked me up from the airport and drove me to the meet-up spot for our overseas program, a hotel near the Seine. We parked the car nearby so that we could have a nice lunch together first, and as I stepped out of the car onto the street, I saw you again. You were by yourself, walking without your bags, perhaps just killing time before our group started the formal welcome activities. We spoke for a brief minute, on what topic I have forgotten, because I had all of a sudden became quite shy to see you again. Later on, as I nibbled on some bread at lunch, my aunt asked about you. I told her you were sympa, but to me, you were without a doubt the coolest guy I had ever met.

## August 12th, 2012
> "I'm not going to get to see Stevie Wonder, am I?"

As I come to I immediately realize that I am in an ambulance. The paramedic next to me is talking to the driver. I try to turn and see them but I realize that my head has been stabilized. I glance toward my feet, feeling a sharp pain emanating from my right big toe. I can taste blood in the corners of my mouth and when I run my younger along my teeth I feel extra space near my front tooth. I start speaking to get the paramedic to take the oxygen mask of my face. Once it's removed he asks me if I know who I am. I pause, then respond, "i'm not going to get to see Stevie Wonder am I? The paramedic smiles and replies "No, probably not. But you're alive so it's not all bad". We share an awkward laugh. We were on our way to SF general hospital.

## Sep 2016 Temple Burn
Last year I watched the temple burn from the passenger seat. At the time i was proud of myself for the traffic that was being beat. This year I sat in a small circle of close friends and watched a beautiful structure burned to the ground as had been done previous nights. As the structure raised I could hear the clicks of camera shutters slowly begin to fade into the sounds of gentle sobs and heavy sighs. Then something started to happen within me. Like oxygen escaping the logs that burned before me an uncontrollable sorrow began seeping out of me from a place so deep . the depths of which I was previously unaware. And then I started to think about all the shit I've got to cry about."}]],"sections":[[10,0]],"ghostVersion":"3.0"}
