I grew up with three brothers under the protection of my maternal grandmother Johnnie Mae Drake. Like most children our age we created trouble on a regular basis. I'll never forget one day when I was around the age of 5 or 6 my brother Brandon (who was a year older) and I did something particularly mischievous that elicited a response from Johnnie Mae that we had never seen. Usually filled with rage at the moment she realizes that we've broken something, or we had otherwise irked her nerves, this time she exhibited a calm that struck fear in our hearts. She called us into her room and tools is to sit down. Considering she normally forbade us from entering her room this only made us more terrified. She pointed to a wardrobe in the corner of her room.

"Inside of that wardrobe is where Betsy lives. When I was a little girl and I did something bad my mother would typically ask me to go fetch her a switch from a tree to get a speaking with. One day, I was housing around in the kitchen and knocked all of the plates in the house to the ground broke them into a million pieces. My mother was so mad at this she didn't send for a switch this time. You see my mother has some Native American blood in her and so she got a lot of old clothes and belongings passed down for generations. One such belonging was this old belt that her mother had made. The belt was beautiful h thick hand stitched buffalo hide that was beautiful to wear, but served another more sinister purpose. You see getting a whopping with this belt wasn't like getting hit with a normal belt. It had holes precisely drilled along the middle to cut down on wind resistance. The stitching was old and frayed to the point that you could cut yourself at parts. And midst importantly it has lashes at both ends. When my mother brought that belt out I knew it was gonna hurt, but I could never be ready for the pain I would feel when Betsy finally touched my backside. The holes and the lashes in the belt made this sickening howling noise that would blend in with my howls accompanying every lash.I don't know how long the

December 9, 1994 Johnnie Mae

  • Fell sick one day before school
  • Not allowed to visit her in the hospital
  • Suffered cardiac arrest and went into a coma
  • Came home from school and was asked to sit on her bed by Aunt D.D.
  • Knew immediately she had passed
  • Will never forget driving down the street with my dad and Ryan spotting Chellye on the street.

Unknown Date: Robbins, IL

For the second time in the last few days I have been overcome with the desire to hear gospel music. I thumbed through my collection to see what music was at my perusal only to realize that I probably haven't added a new Gospel song to my library in years. Fortunately Gospel music is eternal (no pun intended) and the message and chord progressions don't change much over time. I started thinking about how pathetic R&B music has become and I started to wonder if Gospel would have suffered a similar devastation of talent by the ever-popular "autotune". I have become sidetracked. My original purpose in opening in my laptop was to start writing. I don't think a shred of an idea of what I will write but I am convinced that my new method of just writing the first thing that comes to my mind. It's quite ironic that I am trying this for the first time right now because I constantly recommend it to students that I assist with essays. I like to read fluid thought. I always request that they not edit anything that they send me, and to try their best not to preemptively come up with something to write. The mind is an incredibly capable tool that when let loose on paper can produce something much more complex and impressive than what our conscious minds can produce. I feel that sometimes the best thing to do is to take your mind out of the equation, put on some inspirational music, close your eyes and let your fingers directly translate whatever comes into your mind only as quickly as you can only to be interrupted by the occasional wine break. You may want to open your eyes though during the wine break because I dropped half a glass of red wine in my lap earlier and will smell like wine all day now... nice.

I want to discuss my grandmother. Not Ruth, but Johnnie Mae. If anyone asks me I will always say that her death was one of the most defining moments in my life, yet I don't talk much about the woman herself. I think the reason that I avoid the subject of my grandmother is that focusing on her shines a light on one of my biggest shames. I don't really remember the woman. I know that it was her heroic actions that are responsible for me being where I am today. Actions that were heroic in the most precise definition of the word: by saving my three brothers and I my grandmother ultimately sacrificed herself, a debt that I consider unpaid even today.

My earliest memories are from living in Robbins, IL. I can remember flashes of things earlier than my childhood in Robbins but these images are just: brief flashes of seemingly random moments that have no coherence or significance that I can understand. It is for this reason that I consider my memory to start with one consistent and motion picture memory. I am in the enormous backyard of our home. This is my grandmother's home and it is a beautiful one. The front yard was immense with a short fence at the head. I don't remember the fence being particularly aesthetically pleasing but we were the only one of our neighbors that had one and I considered this an accomplishment. There was not much of a porch but that did not prevent my grandmother from sitting out on those three steps and doing whatever it was that grandma did. From my memory it was three stories high with a basement that was accessible to the left as soon as you entered the front door. The basement was where my brothers and I were allowed to play with our toys, naturally making it our favorite room. It was often the room that had the washer and dryer in it, and naturally my brothers and I would take time getting in the dryer, but we had no clue how to turn it on so I assume today that some precautions were taken to keep us from figuring it out. My youngest brother Ryan could not play with us because he was only 1 at the time and Brandon and I were notorious for playing pretty rough. One of my mother's favorite stories back then and even today when she left the two of us together when I was one year old and Brandon was 2 and he put me in a shoebox and sat on me and told her that I was gone... I never found humor in this story. Brandon would actually talk to Ryan the same way that he talked to me. This I never understood because no matter how simply I spoke to Ryan all I ever got was incoherent gurgling and squeaking. I already thought Ryan was a strange looking baby. He had irregularly large eyes and a lot of curly jet-black hair like a baby Jimi Hendrix, not that I had any clue who Jimi was at the time. Brandon spoke to him though and I just stood there and waited for him to be done most of the time.

My room was incredible. It was on the third floor with long windows that allowed so much sun into the room during the day that I was under the impression that it was so high up that it was closer to the sun. This thought entertained me at night when I thought about opening the window one morning and blocking the sun from everyone else. Ma and grandma never left the windows open when they were not in the room with me because they were well aware of my adventurousness. Back in these days Brandon and I did absolutely nothing. We didn't play any sports, we didn't care to read anything, we didn't like carnivals or circuses. Well technically I did like circuses but one time a donkey at a small circus stepped on Brandon's toe and he refused to ever go back to anything that was even close to a circus. What we spent our time doing was largely just using our imagination and getting into trouble. Our playground to us the most magnificent place on earth: the backyard. First of this entire backyard was enormous in proportion to the rest of the house. But it wasn't just the space that made this place incredible. Sitting in the backyard was also an enormous boat that was not in use at the time and belonged to our Uncle Joe who owned the cleaners near our home. I remember my first visit to his dingy little cleaners mainly because he had a gigantic fish tank. After seeing it for the first time I was determined to get a pet to accompany Brandon on our adventures in the backyard. I must have been a persuasive kid because not too soon after the visit we had a new dog that we affectionately named Poochie. So back to the backyard, we have a gigantic boat and a broken down Cadillac. There are bushes to the left that sprout some little berry that grandma convinces us is poisonous. Not only do we not eat the berries, we don't even get near them.

Brandon and I played a wide assortment of games, most of which were pioneered by us right there on the spot. While I truly wish that I remember enough of the details of these doubtlessly complex and entertaining games most of that escapes me. What I do know is that we loved hide and seek. In this backyard it became a particularly tough game for an assortment of reasons. First, the entire backyard was game for hiding (except of course the berry bushes because you would die). Second, neither Brandon or I had the attention span to search for very long, so if you hid somewhere really good chances are the seeker would quit without telling you and just walk away, leaving you to hide for an undetermined amount of time in whatever uncomfortable place you are. You may not be able to read the contempt in my voice through these pages but that often happened to me. One day in particular I was hiding in some bushes near the house and there was faucet nearby. This was a particularly hot summer day and many mosquitoes were attracted to this area. I realized that it was a bad place to be in for a long time as soon as I got there but thought that finding me would be easy and so I would not be there long. You see with Brandon and my version of hide and seek the goal was not to really hide forever but to be caught by trying your best to scare the other person shitless when they came nearby. So when Brandon was in the area I got excited that he would get close enough for me to scare him but instead he wandered into the woods nearer the house. I decided that I could bare it for a little bit longer and sat there. I have no idea how long I was there but when I left the bush I was absolutely covered in mosquito bites. I was itching so badly I had no choice but to see grandma. She was much less than pleased at my state and rapped me a few times upside the head for it. I kept complaining that Brandon didn't look for me and made me stay there and she only would reply with "Stop being a tattletale" or "Brandon's not covered in mosquito bites is he? Did he put a gun to your head and make you do it?". These kinds of things annoyed me to the utmost and I never wanted to be around grandma when she was like that. She clearly favored Brandon above me and I could not determine why. Wanting my ma I asked her 'where is mama?' She replied that she was out with my father and this phrase struck me as very odd. I understood the concept of what a father but was not at all aware that I had one. Apparently I had lived with my dad the first few years of my life before my parents divorced right before Ryan was born. Naturally, I was curious to know more about this man and proceeded with a million questions for my grandmother. It was not long before she seemed frustrated, the first indication that I ever had of the animosity between my grandma and father, this man that I had never actually met.

I obsessed over the memories flash images in my head searching for his face in any of these fuzzy memories. There was one man that I vaguely remember. This man was really tall, strong, with a baldhead and an earring. I never could put my finger on exactly where this man came from in my memories but I had an undeniable feeling that this man was my father. I committed that image to memory so that the next time I saw this man I would not make the mistake of missing him. I was obsessed with the possibility that I would see this man soon and I told Brandon. He immediately began to brag that he knew our father. He knew what he looked like and how his voice sounded and more details. I could not wait to finally meet this man, but it would not happen that night and in fact would not happen for a long time. And the image that I had been holding on to I soon realized was not my father at all, but that of Michael Jordan. I was incredibly disappointed in this discovery. I didn't understand why I had never met this man and so it was easy for me to let go as a kid.

April 8, 2006

Harvey Illinois
I was born in Chicago Illinois in 1988. Early in my life my family moved around a lot between different neighborhoods on Chicago's south side, west side and south suburbs (namely Robbins, Harvey and Midlothian). Subsequently, I've known some pretty rough neighborhoods. This only changed when between 1994-1997 I lived with my grandmother in a small relatively safe neighborhood in the Roseland area until she passed away just before the Christmas of '97. As a result I moved from the south side to live with my paternal grandmother in Harvey. Having lived there briefly years before I was well aware of the dangers that lurked in the city: plagued with all of the same crimes as the city but with a fraction of the police force, there was a general sense that the drug dealers and gangsters were the ones really in charge in this town of just over 25 thousand residents. The local high school was

  • Mayor Eric Kellogg named "Top White Collar Criminal"
  • Former City Spokesman calls suburb "dangerous"
  • Justice Department Investigation into HPD
  • City of Harvey: Be ashamed
  • Warzone in Harvey
  • Alvarado sues city
  • Lawless Harvey

Misuse of funds

  • Kellogg paid off election fines with strip club and gas station money
  • Kellogg settled the city's outstanding debt to the city of Chicago but it is unclear how the city will afford the more than $200k/monthly bill while operating at a deficit.
  • The city of Harvey is paying $1.4 million to settle a lawsuit accusing Mayor Eric Kellogg and a police detective of sending a man to prison because they wrongly thought he stole cocaine from them.

Corruption

  • Five relatives are on the city’s payroll – including a $170,000 position to his niece as city attorney. He, himself, was double dipping as mayor and School Superintendent earning him an additional $202,000 for the latter.
  • In 2006, Mayor Kellogg ordered a police detective to take a gun seized from a convicted drug felon and make it “vanish” from evidence (Daily Southtown).
  • The son of a former school board president who was convicted of fraud for stealing $10,000 in student aid money from the State of Illinois – was rehired by Mayor Kellogg and the City of Harvard. (Chicago Tribune).
  • A Harvey mayoral candidate running against Mayor Kellogg was falsely arrested for political gains.
  • The Illinois Press Association put the City of Harvey third on their list of “worst offenders in the state when it comes to the Illinois Open Meetings Act and the Illinois Freedom of Information Act” (Daily Southtown)
  • Mayor Kellogg and the City of Harvey for paying Mustapha Farrakhan, son of Nation of Islam leader Louis Farrakhan, for part-time police work. The young Farrakhan has a police badge, drives an unmarked Harvey Police squad car, and is registered with the state to carry a gun as an official City of Harvey policeman. Yet Mustapha Farrakhan hasn’t worked a single shift in four years.

April 1, 2009

As I make my way through the terminal at O'hare airport I am relieved that my hangover from the previous night has finally subsided just in time for my trans-Atlantic flight. Believe it or not, this hangover is a badge of honor, a relic of my first legal night of drinking. Back home in Chicago for the first time in over a year I had been afraid that I would have spent the night alone. With Stanford on the quarter schedule my spring break didn't coincide with that of most of my friends from high school. So, I texted around to find a drinking mate for the occasion and was lucky enough to get a response from an old crush who I had run cross country with in high school and who went to school in the city. We hadn't been in touch much since but were both eager to catch up. We wound up at a super divy karaoke bar on the north side called the Blue Frog where the bartender took extra amusement in denying serving me until the clock struck midnight.
When the time finally came she served me an obnoxiously alcoholic special off the menu which lethally combined about 5 different liquors in a way so fruity and delicious that a seven year old would love it for about 5 mins before blacking out. I only handled this drink slightly better than a seven year old would, and less than an hour later had downed four and was doing some serious pelvic thrusting while singing "Bump & Grind" on the stage. My female accomplice was apparently impressed enough with my moves that we were soon making out in between gulping our viciously alcoholic drinks, but to my dismay that's as far as I could swing it that night. Reflecting back on my exploits as I boarded the plane I resolved to finish what I had started with her last night the next time I was in Chicago, assuming its not another few years before we saw each other again.
Once I've taken my seat on the plane and the flight attendants start making announcements in English and French my excitement for my destination begins to escalate. In less than eight hours I'll be touching down in my home for the next three or so months, the City of Lights. Studying abroad in Paris had been eagerly awaiting since I started college, and although I unfortunately had to scrap my plans to study abroad for the winter quarter, nothing could have prevented me from spending my spring in Paris. A few rows up I notice a classmate of mine undoubtedly heading to the same place: at 6'6" Jeff is unmissable in a crowd, and we'd gotten to know each other pretty well from classes that we had taken together in the Stanford drama department. I start to wonder to myself how many other Stanford students might be on this flight, we're on a 757 jet and its a packed flight so I can only see a small portion of the people on the plane. The seat next to me is still unoccupied and I wonder if I could be lucky enough to be the only person to get a section to myself. Im in the row to the left of the plane with only two seats per row.
Predictably, someone gets to the row and stops to sit down. I can't exactly put my finger on her age, but she can't be older than me. Long curly brown hair hangs out of the heather grey hood that she has drawn over her head. She's wearing an outfit that is obviously comfortable for the long flight, but nonetheless stylish: her blue skirt matches well with the tights that she's wearing which have this unique large V pattern that seems to be intended to direct my eyes down her legs to the grey 2 inch heels she's got on. In one of the most understated ways, this young lady is drop dead gorgeious,and she takes her seat without saying anything. I glance over at her quickly, but she just looks ahead. So as not to be seen staring at her out of her peripheral vision I just get back to reading my book and listening to Amos Lee in my headphones. I assume this girl is french, mainly because of her style and figure my chances of wooing a french girl before I even land on the continent to be pretty bad.
After 15 minutes of waiting to taxi the attendant gets on the speaker to inform us that the runways are very backed up and it can be as long as an hour before we're able to take off. A predictable groan reverberates throughout the plane. My seat mate simply sighs and continues flipping through a magazine. After a few minutes I hear her strike up a conversation with a couple across the aisle in French so good that it initially confirms my suspicions that she is returning home. It's only once I start eavesdropping on their conversation (understanding as much as I can with my limited french and headphones over one of my ears) that I am surprised to find out that she is actually from Los Angeles. I get intrigued and take the headphone off completely to better hear. Soon she explains to them that she is on her way to Paris to study abroad and it finally occurs to me that she has to be in my program (since as I noted earlier most school's are on a different schedule). At first this is startling to me, because Stanford students study abroad in their junior year which would mean that she was in my class and I had never met or even seen her before. This seems unlikely after further consideration and I decide that she must be a senior or sophomore. I soon overhear her mention Stanford and inside my heart leaps at the confirmation. Prior to this I had carefully studied the photos of the girls in the program looking for the cutest and had not noticed this one. Perhaps, she was a late addition or just took a very terrible passport photo. I almost start to pull out my packet and determine which it is, but realize that I currently have the advantage that she doesn't seem to know that I'm in her program and decide that I will use this to my advantage.
A few minutes pass and her conversation across the aisle ends. I wait a bit and then gently start with an "excusez moi, but I couldn't help overhearing..." Soon we're conversing and she's largely repeating to me in English what she had told the couple across the aisle. Like most Stanford students she is reluctant to say where she goes to school and only says that she's from Los Angeles but goes to a school in "Northern California" (the moment after people find out that you go to Stanford is always slightly embarrassing and awkward because they always assume that you're far smarter than you feel). I'm amused that she has no idea that I go to Stanford as well and decide to play with this for as long as I can. I am coy and tell her that I was born in Chicago and that my birthday was last night. I interrogate her about herself for a few minutes mixing in some random topics before she asks more questions about myself. And eventually she asks my name and I tell her. For some reason, I say my full name (probably because I'm in love with my name) and notice her pause. It's at this point that I realize that I may have given myself away. Despite the fact that I've never so much as seen this girl in my life Stanford is a small community and there is a decent chance that she has heard my name before. In fact, there's an even greater chance that she has been emailed several times by me because I had been class president the year before. Knowing that the jig is up I confess to her that I am actually in the same program that she's in. She smiles. Luckily she's entertained by my antics and immediately warms up. I ask her name and she replies Yasmine. I repeat it back to her and ask for some clarification, "is it Yaz-min or Yaz-meen?" She looks surprised by the question. "It is yaz-meen, but people can rarely pronounce it correctly so I don't mind either". I go on a short diatribe about how everyone deserves to have their name pronounced correctly... I share stories about bizarre things written on my cup at Starbucks. She seems entertained.
Meanwhile we're still stuck on this runway (it's been over 45 minutes already) and we have little to entertain ourselves but talking to one another. We're covering a wide range of topics none of which I remember because I'm actually slightly hypnotized by the largest, most beautiful kiwi green eyes that I've ever seen. Her pupils are strangely dilated but it still can't take away from the amazing color and depth in her eyes. I try not to look as enthralled as I am, but I get the feeling that she doesn't notice my obsession with her eyes. She's very cordial and all the while I'm trying to figure out how in the world this cutie is in my class and I've never laid eyes on her before. We start comparing the circles that we run in and then it all starts to make sense. She generally runs in quiet, studious and largely asian crowds while my crowd is more of the loud, partying largely greek crowd. We express our excitement for the program. She explains to me that she is Algerian, which is why her French is so impeccable. She has family in Paris, and is excited that her aunt is going to pick her up from the airport when she arrives. I joke that I'll keep her around so that the French don't spit in the food of the guy with the horrendous American accent.
After what seems like an eternity the jet is finally ready to take off. With that our conversation slows. We exchange a few words over the course of the flight about all kinds of subjects like what music we like or what dorms we've lived in, but both of us eventually get self-conscious about potentially over extending the conversation in the event that the other wants to sleep or read or something and at some point I close my eyes and before I know it I am actually asleep. It would not surprise me to awake to find the seat next to me empty, this mysterious, sexy girl only a figment of my imagination or character from a dream. It seems absurd to me but I know that somewhere deep in those green eyes of hers I had fallen in love.

Yasmine's Perspective
As I squeezed through the aisles scanning the numbers for my seat, lugging my packed suitcase along in haste, I spotted you from a few rows back. I recognized you as the guy Jeff was talking to in the boarding line outside. I didn’t know Jeff personally, but knew unmistakably and immediately that he too was from Stanford, having spotted him wandering campus on several occasions in my first three years on the farm. He had been on my previous flight from Los Angeles to Chicago, and now here he was, boarding for Paris. So I knew, too, that you must be from Stanford, and in our study abroad program. I heard you both talking about the Paris program, catching some words here and there as I sat in a seat eating a bagel and pretending to listen to music.

I approached our row and noticed that I’d be sitting next to you. I smiled furtively, enjoying the secret pleasure of knowing just a little bit more than you about the lay of the land. I hoisted my bag into the overhead compartment, climbed into my seat, and said hi, perhaps first, perhaps in response. In your t-shirt, jeans and boat shoes, you had no idea who I was. I was just some girl, and I basked in the mysteriousness of my presence. I quickly plugged in my little iPhone headphones-- nothing compared to the large stereo phones strung around your neck-- and settled into what I expected to be 8 hours of anticipation. But I was quickly interrupted by an older woman, sitting across the aisle from us, who complimented my St.Tropezienne sandals. We began to chat about Paris, she was leading a group of high school students from the midwest on a study abroad program, and she teased out of me that I too would be studying abroad in Paris, through Stanford. Alas, the jig was now up, and you and I suddenly knew the same amount about one another. Assuming of course, that you had overheard my conversation with our row neighbor, just as I had eavesdropped on your chat with Jeff.

And you had. You turned to me and asked if I was also in the Paris program, confirming that you too would be studying there. I feigned surprise. But for those next few hours we spent sitting on the runway in Chicago, for the first time in my life, I was surprised. I was intrigued, I was curious, I was impressed. We spoke about your birthday the day before, your scholarship donor who had an apartment in Paris, the fact that you were our president the year before and I had recognized your name from all those “giving tree” emails. And I kept thinking to myself, over and over, I want to be friends with him. We didn’t talk too much more as the flight took off, both of us trying to pass time watching movies, listening to music, sleeping.

And then we landed in Paris. We went our separate ways after picking up our bags, but not before we were both laughed at by French customs officers for having unnecessary student visas on our American passports. My aunt picked me up from the airport and drove me to the meet-up spot for our overseas program, a hotel near the Seine. We parked the car nearby so that we could have a nice lunch together first, and as I stepped out of the car onto the street, I saw you again. You were by yourself, walking without your bags, perhaps just killing time before our group started the formal welcome activities. We spoke for a brief minute, on what topic I have forgotten, because I had all of a sudden became quite shy to see you again. Later on, as I nibbled on some bread at lunch, my aunt asked about you. I told her you were sympa, but to me, you were without a doubt the coolest guy I had ever met.

August 12th, 2012

"I'm not going to get to see Stevie Wonder, am I?"

As I come to I immediately realize that I am in an ambulance. The paramedic next to me is talking to the driver. I try to turn and see them but I realize that my head has been stabilized. I glance toward my feet, feeling a sharp pain emanating from my right big toe. I can taste blood in the corners of my mouth and when I run my younger along my teeth I feel extra space near my front tooth. I start speaking to get the paramedic to take the oxygen mask of my face. Once it's removed he asks me if I know who I am. I pause, then respond, "i'm not going to get to see Stevie Wonder am I? The paramedic smiles and replies "No, probably not. But you're alive so it's not all bad". We share an awkward laugh. We were on our way to SF general hospital.

Sep 2016 Temple Burn

Last year I watched the temple burn from the passenger seat. At the time i was proud of myself for the traffic that was being beat. This year I sat in a small circle of close friends and watched a beautiful structure burned to the ground as had been done previous nights. As the structure raised I could hear the clicks of camera shutters slowly begin to fade into the sounds of gentle sobs and heavy sighs. Then something started to happen within me. Like oxygen escaping the logs that burned before me an uncontrollable sorrow began seeping out of me from a place so deep . the depths of which I was previously unaware. And then I started to think about all the shit I've got to cry about.

Johnnie Mae Drake. Like most children our age we created trouble on a regular
basis. I'll never forget one day when I was around the age of 5 or 6 my brother
Brandon (who was a year older) and I did something particularly mischievous that
elicited a response from Johnnie Mae that we had never seen. Usually filled with
rage at the moment she realizes that we've broken something, or we had otherwise
irked her nerves, this time she exhibited a calm that struck fear in our hearts.
She called us into her room and tools is to sit down. Considering she normally
forbade us from entering her room this only made us more terrified. She pointed
to a wardrobe in the corner of her room.

"Inside of that wardrobe is where Betsy lives. When I was a little girl and I
did something bad my mother would typically ask me to go fetch her a switch from
a tree to get a speaking with. One day, I was housing around in the kitchen and
knocked all of the plates in the house to the ground broke them into a million
pieces. My mother was so mad at this she didn't send for a switch this time. You
see my mother has some Native American blood in her and so she got a lot of old

Unknown Date: Robbins, IL
For the second time in the last few days I have been overcome with the desire to
hear gospel music. I thumbed through my collection to see what music was at my
perusal only to realize that I probably haven't added a new Gospel song to my
library in years. Fortunately Gospel music is eternal (no pun intended) and the
message and chord progressions don't change much over time. I started thinking
about how pathetic R&B music has become and I started to wonder if Gospel would
have suffered a similar devastation of talent by the ever-popular "autotune". I
have become sidetracked. My original purpose in opening in my laptop was to
start writing. I don't think a shred of an idea of what I will write but I am
convinced that my new method of just writing the first thing that comes to my
mind. It's quite ironic that I am trying this for the first time right now
because I constantly recommend it to students that I assist with essays. I like
to read fluid thought. I always request that they not edit anything that they
send me, and to try their best not to preemptively come up with something to
write. The mind is an incredibly capable tool that when let loose on paper can
produce something much more complex and impressive than what our conscious minds
can produce. I feel that sometimes the best thing to do is to take your mind out
of the equation, put on some inspirational music, close your eyes and let your
fingers directly translate whatever comes into your mind only as quickly as you
can only to be interrupted by the occasional wine break. You may want to open
your eyes though during the wine break because I dropped half a glass of red
wine in my lap earlier and will smell like wine all day now... nice.

I want to discuss my grandmother. Not Ruth, but Johnnie Mae. If anyone asks me I
will always say that her death was one of the most defining moments in my life,
yet I don't talk much about the woman herself. I think the reason that I avoid
the subject of my grandmother is that focusing on her shines a light on one of
my biggest shames. I don't really remember the woman. I know that it was her
heroic actions that are responsible for me being where I am today. Actions that
were heroic in the most precise definition of the word: by saving my three
brothers and I my grandmother ultimately sacrificed herself, a debt that I
consider unpaid even today.

My earliest memories are from living in Robbins, IL. I can remember flashes of
things earlier than my childhood in Robbins but these images are just: brief
flashes of seemingly random moments that have no coherence or significance that
I can understand. It is for this reason that I consider my memory to start with
one consistent and motion picture memory. I am in the enormous backyard of our
home. This is my grandmother's home and it is a beautiful one. The front yard
was immense with a short fence at the head. I don't remember the fence being
particularly aesthetically pleasing but we were the only one of our neighbors
that had one and I considered this an accomplishment. There was not much of a
porch but that did not prevent my grandmother from sitting out on those three
steps and doing whatever it was that grandma did. From my memory it was three
stories high with a basement that was accessible to the left as soon as you
entered the front door. The basement was where my brothers and I were allowed to
play with our toys, naturally making it our favorite room. It was often the room
that had the washer and dryer in it, and naturally my brothers and I would take
time getting in the dryer, but we had no clue how to turn it on so I assume
today that some precautions were taken to keep us from figuring it out. My
youngest brother Ryan could not play with us because he was only 1 at the time
and Brandon and I were notorious for playing pretty rough. One of my mother's
favorite stories back then and even today when she left the two of us together
when I was one year old and Brandon was 2 and he put me in a shoebox and sat on
me and told her that I was gone... I never found humor in this story. Brandon
would actually talk to Ryan the same way that he talked to me. This I never
understood because no matter how simply I spoke to Ryan all I ever got was
incoherent gurgling and squeaking. I already thought Ryan was a strange looking
baby. He had irregularly large eyes and a lot of curly jet-black hair like a
baby Jimi Hendrix, not that I had any clue who Jimi was at the time. Brandon
spoke to him though and I just stood there and waited for him to be done most of
the time.

My room was incredible. It was on the third floor with long windows that allowed
so much sun into the room during the day that I was under the impression that it
was so high up that it was closer to the sun. This thought entertained me at
night when I thought about opening the window one morning and blocking the sun
from everyone else. Ma and grandma never left the windows open when they were
not in the room with me because they were well aware of my adventurousness. Back
in these days Brandon and I did absolutely nothing. We didn't play any sports,
we didn't care to read anything, we didn't like carnivals or circuses. Well
technically I did like circuses but one time a donkey at a small circus stepped
on Brandon's toe and he refused to ever go back to anything that was even close
to a circus. What we spent our time doing was largely just using our imagination
and getting into trouble. Our playground to us the most magnificent place on
earth: the backyard. First of this entire backyard was enormous in proportion to
the rest of the house. But it wasn't just the space that made this place
incredible. Sitting in the backyard was also an enormous boat that was not in
use at the time and belonged to our Uncle Joe who owned the cleaners near our
home. I remember my first visit to his dingy little cleaners mainly because he
had a gigantic fish tank. After seeing it for the first time I was determined to
get a pet to accompany Brandon on our adventures in the backyard. I must have
been a persuasive kid because not too soon after the visit we had a new dog that
we affectionately named Poochie. So back to the backyard, we have a gigantic
boat and a broken down Cadillac. There are bushes to the left that sprout some
little berry that grandma convinces us is poisonous. Not only do we not eat the
berries, we don't even get near them.

Brandon and I played a wide assortment of games, most of which were pioneered by
us right there on the spot. While I truly wish that I remember enough of the
details of these doubtlessly complex and entertaining games most of that escapes
me. What I do know is that we loved hide and seek. In this backyard it became a
particularly tough game for an assortment of reasons. First, the entire backyard
was game for hiding (except of course the berry bushes because you would die).
Second, neither Brandon or I had the attention span to search for very long, so
if you hid somewhere really good chances are the seeker would quit without
telling you and just walk away, leaving you to hide for an undetermined amount
of time in whatever uncomfortable place you are. You may not be able to read the
contempt in my voice through these pages but that often happened to me. One day
in particular I was hiding in some bushes near the house and there was faucet
nearby. This was a particularly hot summer day and many mosquitoes were
attracted to this area. I realized that it was a bad place to be in for a long
time as soon as I got there but thought that finding me would be easy and so I
would not be there long. You see with Brandon and my version of hide and seek
the goal was not to really hide forever but to be caught by trying your best to
scare the other person shitless when they came nearby. So when Brandon was in
the area I got excited that he would get close enough for me to scare him but
instead he wandered into the woods nearer the house. I decided that I could bare
it for a little bit longer and sat there. I have no idea how long I was there
but when I left the bush I was absolutely covered in mosquito bites. I was
itching so badly I had no choice but to see grandma. She was much less than
pleased at my state and rapped me a few times upside the head for it. I kept
complaining that Brandon didn't look for me and made me stay there and she only
would reply with "Stop being a tattletale" or "Brandon's not covered in mosquito
bites is he? Did he put a gun to your head and make you do it?". These kinds of
things annoyed me to the utmost and I never wanted to be around grandma when she
was like that. She clearly favored Brandon above me and I could not determine
why. Wanting my ma I asked her 'where is mama?' She replied that she was out
with my father and this phrase struck me as very odd. I understood the concept
of what a father but was not at all aware that I had one. Apparently I had lived
with my dad the first few years of my life before my parents divorced right
before Ryan was born. Naturally, I was curious to know more about this man and
proceeded with a million questions for my grandmother. It was not long before
she seemed frustrated, the first indication that I ever had of the animosity
between my grandma and father, this man that I had never actually met.

I obsessed over the memories flash images in my head searching for his face in
any of these fuzzy memories. There was one man that I vaguely remember. This man
was really tall, strong, with a baldhead and an earring. I never could put my
finger on exactly where this man came from in my memories but I had an
undeniable feeling that this man was my father. I committed that image to memory
so that the next time I saw this man I would not make the mistake of missing
him. I was obsessed with the possibility that I would see this man soon and I
told Brandon. He immediately began to brag that he knew our father. He knew what
he looked like and how his voice sounded and more details. I could not wait to
finally meet this man, but it would not happen that night and in fact would not
happen for a long time. And the image that I had been holding on to I soon
realized was not my father at all, but that of Michael Jordan. I was incredibly
disappointed in this discovery. I didn't understand why I had never met this man
and so it was easy for me to let go as a kid.

April 8, 2006
Harvey Illinois [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harvey,_Illinois]
I was born in Chicago Illinois in 1988. Early in my life my family moved around
a lot between different neighborhoods on Chicago's south side, west side and
south suburbs (namely Robbins, Harvey and Midlothian). Subsequently, I've known
some pretty rough neighborhoods. This only changed when between 1994-1997 I
lived with my grandmother in a small relatively safe neighborhood in the
Roseland area until she passed away just before the Christmas of '97. As a
result I moved from the south side to live with my paternal grandmother in
Harvey. Having lived there briefly years before I was well aware of the dangers
that lurked in the city: plagued with all of the same crimes as the city but
with a fraction of the police force, there was a general sense that the drug
dealers and gangsters were the ones really in charge in this town of just over
25 thousand residents. The local high school was

 * Mayor Eric Kellogg named "Top White Collar Criminal"
   [http://www.truecrimereport.com/2010/11/top_5_white_collar_villains.php]
 * Former City Spokesman calls suburb "dangerous"
   [http://articles.chicagotribune.com/2014-05-27/news/ct-harvey-aide-quits-met-20140526_1_sandra-alvarado-mayor-eric-kellogg-suburb]
 * Justice Department Investigation into HPD
   [http://www.justice.gov/crt/about/spl/documents/harvey_findings_1-18-12.pdf]
 * City of Harvey: Be ashamed
   [https://mrmiller23.wordpress.com/2013/04/23/city-of-harvey-be-ashamed/]
 * Warzone in Harvey
   [http://southsuburbanwire.blogspot.com/2013/02/warzone-in-harvey.html]
 * Alvarado sues city
   [http://www.chicagotribune.com/news/local/breaking/ct-harvey-discrimination-lawsuit-met-20150410-story.html]
 * Lawless Harvey
   [http://www.chicagotribune.com/news/watchdog/ct-harvey-residents-frustrated-met-20141225-story.html#page=1]

Misuse of funds
 * Kellogg paid off election fines with strip club and gas station money
   [http://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-politics/7/71/350585/strip-club-frozen-food-gas-stations-linked-harvey-mayors-bailout]
 * Kellogg settled the city's outstanding debt
   [http://www.chicagotribune.com/suburbs/ct-harvey-chicago-water-met-20150120-story.html] 
   to the city of Chicago but it is unclear how the city will afford the more
   than $200k/monthly bill while operating at a deficit.
 * The city of Harvey is paying $1.4 million to settle a lawsuit accusing Mayor
   Eric Kellogg and a police detective of sending a man to prison
   [http://www.prweb.com/releases/2010/03/prweb3726954.htm] because they wrongly
   thought he stole cocaine from them.

Corruption


 * Five relatives are on the city’s payroll – including a $170,000 position to
   his niece as city attorney. He, himself, was double dipping as mayor and
   School Superintendent earning him an additional $202,000 for the latter.
 * In 2006, Mayor Kellogg ordered a police detective to take a gun seized from a
   convicted drug felon and make it “vanish” from evidence (Daily Southtown).
 * The son of a former school board president who was convicted of fraud for
   stealing $10,000 in student aid money from the State of Illinois – was
   rehired by Mayor Kellogg and the City of Harvard. (Chicago Tribune).
 * A Harvey mayoral candidate running against Mayor Kellogg was falsely arrested
   for political gains.
 * The Illinois Press Association put the City of Harvey third on their list of
   “worst offenders in the state when it comes to the Illinois Open Meetings Act
   and the Illinois Freedom of Information Act” (Daily Southtown)
 * Mayor Kellogg and the City of Harvey for paying Mustapha Farrakhan, son of
   Nation of Islam leader Louis Farrakhan, for part-time police work. The young
   Farrakhan has a police badge, drives an unmarked Harvey Police squad car, and
   is registered with the state to carry a gun as an official City of Harvey
   policeman. Yet Mustapha Farrakhan hasn’t worked a single shift in four years
   [http://www.theblaze.com/stories/2013/04/22/why-is-farrakhans-cop-son-driving-a-taxpayer-funded-police-car-and-carrying-a-gun-when-he-hasnt-worked-a-shift-in-4-years/]
   .

April 1, 2009
As I make my way through the terminal at O'hare airport I am relieved that my
hangover from the previous night has finally subsided just in time for my
trans-Atlantic flight. Believe it or not, this hangover is a badge of honor, a
relic of my first legal night of drinking. Back home in Chicago for the first
time in over a year I had been afraid that I would have spent the night alone.
With Stanford on the quarter schedule my spring break didn't coincide with that
of most of my friends from high school. So, I texted around to find a drinking
mate for the occasion and was lucky enough to get a response from an old crush
who I had run cross country with in high school and who went to school in the
city. We hadn't been in touch much since but were both eager to catch up. We
wound up at a super divy karaoke bar on the north side called the Blue Frog
where the bartender took extra amusement in denying serving me until the clock
struck midnight.
When the time finally came she served me an obnoxiously alcoholic special off
the menu which lethally combined about 5 different liquors in a way so fruity
and delicious that a seven year old would love it for about 5 mins before
blacking out. I only handled this drink slightly better than a seven year old
would, and less than an hour later had downed four and was doing some serious
pelvic thrusting while singing "Bump & Grind" on the stage. My female accomplice
was apparently impressed enough with my moves that we were soon making out in
between gulping our viciously alcoholic drinks, but to my dismay that's as far
as I could swing it that night. Reflecting back on my exploits as I boarded the
plane I resolved to finish what I had started with her last night the next time
I was in Chicago, assuming its not another few years before we saw each other
again.
Once I've taken my seat on the plane and the flight attendants start making
announcements in English and French my excitement for my destination begins to
escalate. In less than eight hours I'll be touching down in my home for the next
three or so months, the City of Lights. Studying abroad in Paris had been
eagerly awaiting since I started college, and although I unfortunately had to
scrap my plans to study abroad for the winter quarter, nothing could have
prevented me from spending my spring in Paris. A few rows up I notice a
classmate of mine undoubtedly heading to the same place: at 6'6" Jeff is
unmissable in a crowd, and we'd gotten to know each other pretty well from
classes that we had taken together in the Stanford drama department. I start to
wonder to myself how many other Stanford students might be on this flight, we're
on a 757 jet and its a packed flight so I can only see a small portion of the
people on the plane. The seat next to me is still unoccupied and I wonder if I
could be lucky enough to be the only person to get a section to myself. Im in
the row to the left of the plane with only two seats per row.
Predictably, someone gets to the row and stops to sit down. I can't exactly put
my finger on her age, but she can't be older than me. Long curly brown hair
hangs out of the heather grey hood that she has drawn over her head. She's
wearing an outfit that is obviously comfortable for the long flight, but
nonetheless stylish: her blue skirt matches well with the tights that she's
wearing which have this unique large V pattern that seems to be intended to
direct my eyes down her legs to the grey 2 inch heels she's got on. In one of
the most understated ways, this young lady is drop dead gorgeious,and she takes
her seat without saying anything. I glance over at her quickly, but she just
looks ahead. So as not to be seen staring at her out of her peripheral vision I
just get back to reading my book and listening to Amos Lee in my headphones. I
assume this girl is french, mainly because of her style and figure my chances of
wooing a french girl before I even land on the continent to be pretty bad.
After 15 minutes of waiting to taxi the attendant gets on the speaker to inform
us that the runways are very backed up and it can be as long as an hour before
we're able to take off. A predictable groan reverberates throughout the plane.
My seat mate simply sighs and continues flipping through a magazine. After a few
minutes I hear her strike up a conversation with a couple across the aisle in
French so good that it initially confirms my suspicions that she is returning
home. It's only once I start eavesdropping on their conversation (understanding
as much as I can with my limited french and headphones over one of my ears) that
I am surprised to find out that she is actually from Los Angeles. I get
intrigued and take the headphone off completely to better hear. Soon she
explains to them that she is on her way to Paris to study abroad and it finally
occurs to me that she has to be in my program (since as I noted earlier most
school's are on a different schedule). At first this is startling to me, because
Stanford students study abroad in their junior year which would mean that she
was in my class and I had never met or even seen her before. This seems unlikely
after further consideration and I decide that she must be a senior or sophomore.
I soon overhear her mention Stanford and inside my heart leaps at the
confirmation. Prior to this I had carefully studied the photos of the girls in
the program looking for the cutest and had not noticed this one. Perhaps, she
was a late addition or just took a very terrible passport photo. I almost start
to pull out my packet and determine which it is, but realize that I currently
have the advantage that she doesn't seem to know that I'm in her program and
decide that I will use this to my advantage.
A few minutes pass and her conversation across the aisle ends. I wait a bit and
then gently start with an "excusez moi, but I couldn't help overhearing..." Soon
we're conversing and she's largely repeating to me in English what she had told
the couple across the aisle. Like most Stanford students she is reluctant to say
where she goes to school and only says that she's from Los Angeles but goes to a
school in "Northern California" (the moment after people find out that you go to
Stanford is always slightly embarrassing and awkward because they always assume
that you're far smarter than you feel). I'm amused that she has no idea that I
go to Stanford as well and decide to play with this for as long as I can. I am
coy and tell her that I was born in Chicago and that my birthday was last night.
I interrogate her about herself for a few minutes mixing in some random topics
before she asks more questions about myself. And eventually she asks my name and
I tell her. For some reason, I say my full name (probably because I'm in love
with my name) and notice her pause. It's at this point that I realize that I may
have given myself away. Despite the fact that I've never so much as seen this
girl in my life Stanford is a small community and there is a decent chance that
she has heard my name before. In fact, there's an even greater chance that she
has been emailed several times by me because I had been class president the year
before. Knowing that the jig is up I confess to her that I am actually in the
same program that she's in. She smiles. Luckily she's entertained by my antics
and immediately warms up. I ask her name and she replies Yasmine. I repeat it
back to her and ask for some clarification, "is it Yaz-min or Yaz-meen?" She
looks surprised by the question. "It is yaz-meen, but people can rarely
pronounce it correctly so I don't mind either". I go on a short diatribe about
how everyone deserves to have their name pronounced correctly... I share stories
about bizarre things written on my cup at Starbucks. She seems entertained.
Meanwhile we're still stuck on this runway (it's been over 45 minutes already)
and we have little to entertain ourselves but talking to one another. We're
covering a wide range of topics none of which I remember because I'm actually
slightly hypnotized by the largest, most beautiful kiwi green eyes that I've
ever seen. Her pupils are strangely dilated but it still can't take away from
the amazing color and depth in her eyes. I try not to look as enthralled as I
am, but I get the feeling that she doesn't notice my obsession with her eyes.
She's very cordial and all the while I'm trying to figure out how in the world
this cutie is in my class and I've never laid eyes on her before. We start
comparing the circles that we run in and then it all starts to make sense. She
generally runs in quiet, studious and largely asian crowds while my crowd is
more of the loud, partying largely greek crowd. We express our excitement for
the program. She explains to me that she is Algerian, which is why her French is
so impeccable. She has family in Paris, and is excited that her aunt is going to
pick her up from the airport when she arrives. I joke that I'll keep her around
so that the French don't spit in the food of the guy with the horrendous
American accent.
After what seems like an eternity the jet is finally ready to take off. With
that our conversation slows. We exchange a few words over the course of the
flight about all kinds of subjects like what music we like or what dorms we've
lived in, but both of us eventually get self-conscious about potentially over
extending the conversation in the event that the other wants to sleep or read or
something and at some point I close my eyes and before I know it I am actually
asleep. It would not surprise me to awake to find the seat next to me empty,
this mysterious, sexy girl only a figment of my imagination or character from a
dream. It seems absurd to me but I know that somewhere deep in those green eyes
of hers I had fallen in love.

Yasmine's Perspective
As I squeezed through the aisles scanning the numbers for my seat, lugging my
packed suitcase along in haste, I spotted you from a few rows back. I recognized
you as the guy Jeff was talking to in the boarding line outside. I didn’t know
Jeff personally, but knew unmistakably and immediately that he too was from
Stanford, having spotted him wandering campus on several occasions in my first
three years on the farm. He had been on my previous flight from Los Angeles to
Chicago, and now here he was, boarding for Paris. So I knew, too, that you must
be from Stanford, and in our study abroad program. I heard you both talking
about the Paris program, catching some words here and there as I sat in a seat
eating a bagel and pretending to listen to music.

I approached our row and noticed that I’d be sitting next to you. I smiled
furtively, enjoying the secret pleasure of knowing just a little bit more than
you about the lay of the land. I hoisted my bag into the overhead compartment,
climbed into my seat, and said hi, perhaps first, perhaps in response. In your
t-shirt, jeans and boat shoes, you had no idea who I was. I was just some girl,
and I basked in the mysteriousness of my presence. I quickly plugged in my
little iPhone headphones-- nothing compared to the large stereo phones strung
around your neck-- and settled into what I expected to be 8 hours of
anticipation. But I was quickly interrupted by an older woman, sitting across
the aisle from us, who complimented my St.Tropezienne sandals. We began to chat
about Paris, she was leading a group of high school students from the midwest on
a study abroad program, and she teased out of me that I too would be studying
abroad in Paris, through Stanford. Alas, the jig was now up, and you and I
suddenly knew the same amount about one another. Assuming of course, that you
had overheard my conversation with our row neighbor, just as I had eavesdropped
on your chat with Jeff.

And you had. You turned to me and asked if I was also in the Paris program,
confirming that you too would be studying there. I feigned surprise. But for
those next few hours we spent sitting on the runway in Chicago, for the first
time in my life, I was surprised. I was intrigued, I was curious, I was
impressed. We spoke about your birthday the day before, your scholarship donor
who had an apartment in Paris, the fact that you were our president the year
before and I had recognized your name from all those “giving tree” emails. And I
kept thinking to myself, over and over, I want to be friends with him. We didn’t
talk too much more as the flight took off, both of us trying to pass time
watching movies, listening to music, sleeping.

And then we landed in Paris. We went our separate ways after picking up our
bags, but not before we were both laughed at by French customs officers for
having unnecessary student visas on our American passports. My aunt picked me up
from the airport and drove me to the meet-up spot for our overseas program, a
hotel near the Seine. We parked the car nearby so that we could have a nice
lunch together first, and as I stepped out of the car onto the street, I saw you
again. You were by yourself, walking without your bags, perhaps just killing
time before our group started the formal welcome activities. We spoke for a
brief minute, on what topic I have forgotten, because I had all of a sudden
became quite shy to see you again. Later on, as I nibbled on some bread at
lunch, my aunt asked about you. I told her you were sympa, but to me, you were
without a doubt the coolest guy I had ever met.

August 16th, 2009
 * Olmstead
 * Robert Moses

August 12th, 2012
> "I'm not going to get to see Stevie Wonder, am I?"


As I come to I immediately realize that I am in an ambulance. The paramedic next
to me is talking to the driver. I try to turn and see them but I realize that my
head has been stabilized. I glance toward my feet, feeling a sharp pain
emanating from my right big toe. I can taste blood in the corners of my mouth
and when I run my younger along my teeth I feel extra space near my front tooth.
I start speaking to get the paramedic to take the oxygen mask of my face. Once
it's removed he asks me if I know who I am. I pause, then respond, "i'm not
going to get to see Stevie Wonder am I? The paramedic smiles and replies "No,
probably not. But you're alive so it's not all bad". We share an awkward laugh.
We were on our way to SF general hospital.

Sep 2016 Temple Burn
Last year I watched the temple burn from the passenger seat. At the time i was
proud of myself for the traffic that was being beat. This year I sat in a small
circle of close friends and watched a beautiful structure burned to the ground
as had been done previous nights. As the structure raised I could hear the
clicks of camera shutters slowly begin to fade into the sounds of gentle sobs
and heavy sighs. Then something started to happen within me. Like oxygen
escaping the logs that burned before me an uncontrollable sorrow began seeping
out of me from a place so deep . the depths of which I was previously unaware.
And then I started to think about all the shit I've got to cry about.

{"version":"0.3.1","markups":[],"atoms":[],"cards":[["markdown",{"cardName":"card-markdown","markdown":"#### Of all the activities, interests, and experiences listed on the previous page, which is the most meaningful to you and why?

 

Every Saturday during the winter months I have to abandon my given name and assume a new identity, randomly assigned to me by anonymous sources. When I enter a speech tournament I am no longer known by a name, but distinguished by a code given to competitors in preliminary rounds. I enjoy this code system. As I examine the competitors listed in my rounds I cannot derive a prior estimation or formulate an opinion about them. Codes cannot in any way distinguish competitors; while names can reveal traits such as gender or ethnicity. I am familiar with being “pre-estimated” in a competition. My competitors take one glance and immediately expect that I am not a major concern for them. Unluckily there is no code system for skin color. As an African American speaker I faced struggles that most of competitors could not comprehend, as I had to constantly disprove the image of poor black language depicted by pop culture, and cancel out years of improper grammar mechanics. My beginning experiences were not exactly pleasant, I felt as if judges were more critical towards me about articulation and grammar than other competitors because they already expected that to be my greatest struggle. As I developed as a speaker I worked harder to rebalance the playing field. Ultimately, it makes no difference what my name is, or my skin colors. The generalization that “black people can't speak” does not have anything to do with me. People formulate preconceptions and set expectations, and it is exceeding that expectation that gives me the greatest satisfaction.

 

#### Sharing Intellectual Interests is an important aspect of university life. Describe an experience or idea that you find intellectually exciting, and explain why.

 

“It has been said something as small as the flutter of a butterfly's wing can ultimately cause a typhoon halfway around the world,”-Chaos theory. The Butterfly Effect refers to Edward Lorenz’s mathematical discovery that over time, minute changes in a complex system can lead to large consequences. My calculus teacher used this theory as a tool to explain the chaotic nature of functions, but it seems logical to assume that what is true for the complexity of functions is equally true for the complex, chaotic social systems we find ourselves immersed in.

Applying this theory was especially invaluable in implementing my own outreach project: shooting documentaries that would increase environmental awareness and care in my community. Film is an opportunity to change the world through the audience, instilling a new and lasting appreciation for our planet. I firmly believe in my ability to transform this planet into a more sustainable place for future generations. Apathy and indifference in my community are difficult tasks to handle, but I used this theory as a tool to inspire and guide the production of my film. When I first rallied the handpicked film crew my first order of business was illustrating how our film could create a change in society. My tool for illustration: a butterfly. We each do our own “flapping”, most of which isn’t nearly as placid as that of a butterfly. We live in an interdependent world in which every person has the power to cause major fluctuations in the dynamic function of life. It is in recognizing this that we can confidently create a positive change. We can only continue flapping so that someone somewhere down the line may enjoy the refreshing breeze.

 

#### Jot a note to your future roommate relating a personal experience that reveals something about you.

 

One detail that you may find indicative of my peculiarity is that I enjoy playing the trumpet, naked. I realized this one-day as I played alongside Michael, a close friend and pianist. We were blazing along the staffs conquering every measure after measure. Unfortunately, this was all ruined once I reached a particularly difficult section laced with key changes and a particularly awkward valve combination. I lost the last note, faltered and destroyed the rhythm of the piece prompting a repeat of the section. Again I attempted to repair the note and again I flubbed. Consequently, Mike suggested that I play naked. Fortunately he continued to explain that by naked, he did not want me to clothe the prodigal note. “You are trying to beat yourself onto this path of what is written on the staffs of this music when really, Cameron Drake’s sound doesn’t exist in between those lines. So take off your clothing and play it over, the way that Cameron would play it.” I had hit plenty of high notes, mastered and executed the song almost flawlessly. That one note did not take away from my sound, it defined my sound. Similarly, in life those notes can go uncorrected in the form of the calculus test that I did not do excellent on this morning or the race that I could not win yesterday. It is in that moment of emotional vulnerability and nakedness that I realized, that which prevents me from being perfect is not my inadequacy, but simply definitions of my character. Those things are not haunting sixteenth notes creating anxiety and disappointment, but are just a part of the beautiful music of my life.

 

 

#### As you reflect on your life thus far, what has someone said, written or expressed in some fashion that is especially meaningful to you? Why?

 

I thought that I was in the wrong place that Thursday night. In reality I could not have been in any better place that night. My church’s substance abuse ministry meeting hosted a weekly outreach meeting for drug addicts and their co-dependents alike. Many were there to try and understand and if fortunate enough, cure a problem that plagued them all. I was there at my pastor’s request to share my experiences with this group. I could relate to the people present at this meeting. My mother had been addicted to drugs for nearly 12 years and is still today. I had to accept the fact that my mother was often missing. Initially it was difficult for me to comprehend why my mother would leave for months or even a year at a time. I pleaded to God nightly to “send my mommy back home”, so often in fact, that I unconsciously said it even when she was at home. My grandmother who raised me through her absence soon gained guardianship over my brothers and me. It was not long before I realized that I was better off without her. I could not survive the abusiveness and lewdness of all of her ‘boyfriends’. I overwhelmingly preferred life with my grandmother. Living with my grandmother was the only way that I could find stability, knowing that I would always have someone to pay attention to me, keep me safe, and prepare dinner every night. I also had its shortcomings. My grandmother was old, too old to keep up with three little boys. When my grandmother died my aunts blamed my brothers and I because we had put her under extreme stress at her old age. They performed their own autopsy and the causes of death were “three nappy headed boys”. They did not care about hurting my feelings; I was just the son of their adopted sister, whom in their opinion had contributed her own fair share to my grandmother’s death. My grandmother’s death also meant that I was without a home, as my mother was missing at the time of her death. No one would take on the ‘burden’ of the three of us. Fortunately my living grandmother decided that she would take us in, and we have lived with her since.

As I shared my experiences I asked myself: How? Viewing my own life experiences objectively for the first time I pondered what was the driving force that allowed me to survive, to become who I am today. I managed to excel academically, despite the fact that I attended nine different schools before the eighth grade. I accepted the fact that my mother had contracted HIV and would eventually die from the disease. I had no parents in the stands cheering when I rounded the track in first place to qualify for state or in the audience when I won speech tournaments because my grandmother was too old to climb into bleachers. How? At that point I wanted to interrupt and tell everyone in that circle exactly what steps I had taken to live despite the poisonous circumstances given. But I could not possibly articulate at that moment what drove me to conquer those hardships. I wanted nothing more than to tell everyone that he/she is not confined to the path that his/her life had molded thus far. I don’t pretend that my past didn’t happen or didn’t affect me. It did happen. I embrace that and draw strength from the experiences that can easily cripple and weaken others. My story is not one to lament over and use as an excuse. I chose to accept the things that I could not change and focus on controlling my life. Furthermore, I was not fortunate enough to endure my hurdles in life just to wear them as an accolade. My story does not serve as a badge of honor to amplify my accomplishments. My story holds answers for people who otherwise would be lost and confused. I want to give them those answers.  I could not do it that night, but I returned weekly since that day. I cannot fix their problems. As an individual I do not know what will work for everyone. I know what worked for me. And if I must relive my negative experiences on a weekly basis, so that someone may find an answer, then I’ll do that.

 "}]],"sections":[[10,0]],"ghostVersion":"3.0"}
posts.html:
Of all the activities, interests, and experiences listed on the previous page, which is the most meaningful to you and why?

Every Saturday during the winter months I have to abandon my given name and assume a new identity, randomly assigned to me by anonymous sources. When I enter a speech tournament I am no longer known by a name, but distinguished by a code given to competitors in preliminary rounds. I enjoy this code system. As I examine the competitors listed in my rounds I cannot derive a prior estimation or formulate an opinion about them. Codes cannot in any way distinguish competitors; while names can reveal traits such as gender or ethnicity. I am familiar with being “pre-estimated” in a competition. My competitors take one glance and immediately expect that I am not a major concern for them. Unluckily there is no code system for skin color. As an African American speaker I faced struggles that most of competitors could not comprehend, as I had to constantly disprove the image of poor black language depicted by pop culture, and cancel out years of improper grammar mechanics. My beginning experiences were not exactly pleasant, I felt as if judges were more critical towards me about articulation and grammar than other competitors because they already expected that to be my greatest struggle. As I developed as a speaker I worked harder to rebalance the playing field. Ultimately, it makes no difference what my name is, or my skin colors. The generalization that “black people can't speak” does not have anything to do with me. People formulate preconceptions and set expectations, and it is exceeding that expectation that gives me the greatest satisfaction.

Sharing Intellectual Interests is an important aspect of university life. Describe an experience or idea that you find intellectually exciting, and explain why.

“It has been said something as small as the flutter of a butterfly's wing can ultimately cause a typhoon halfway around the world,”-Chaos theory. The Butterfly Effect refers to Edward Lorenz’s mathematical discovery that over time, minute changes in a complex system can lead to large consequences. My calculus teacher used this theory as a tool to explain the chaotic nature of functions, but it seems logical to assume that what is true for the complexity of functions is equally true for the complex, chaotic social systems we find ourselves immersed in.

Applying this theory was especially invaluable in implementing my own outreach project: shooting documentaries that would increase environmental awareness and care in my community. Film is an opportunity to change the world through the audience, instilling a new and lasting appreciation for our planet. I firmly believe in my ability to transform this planet into a more sustainable place for future generations. Apathy and indifference in my community are difficult tasks to handle, but I used this theory as a tool to inspire and guide the production of my film. When I first rallied the handpicked film crew my first order of business was illustrating how our film could create a change in society. My tool for illustration: a butterfly. We each do our own “flapping”, most of which isn’t nearly as placid as that of a butterfly. We live in an interdependent world in which every person has the power to cause major fluctuations in the dynamic function of life. It is in recognizing this that we can confidently create a positive change. We can only continue flapping so that someone somewhere down the line may enjoy the refreshing breeze.

Jot a note to your future roommate relating a personal experience that reveals something about you.

One detail that you may find indicative of my peculiarity is that I enjoy playing the trumpet, naked. I realized this one-day as I played alongside Michael, a close friend and pianist. We were blazing along the staffs conquering every measure after measure. Unfortunately, this was all ruined once I reached a particularly difficult section laced with key changes and a particularly awkward valve combination. I lost the last note, faltered and destroyed the rhythm of the piece prompting a repeat of the section. Again I attempted to repair the note and again I flubbed. Consequently, Mike suggested that I play naked. Fortunately he continued to explain that by naked, he did not want me to clothe the prodigal note. “You are trying to beat yourself onto this path of what is written on the staffs of this music when really, Cameron Drake’s sound doesn’t exist in between those lines. So take off your clothing and play it over, the way that Cameron would play it.” I had hit plenty of high notes, mastered and executed the song almost flawlessly. That one note did not take away from my sound, it defined my sound. Similarly, in life those notes can go uncorrected in the form of the calculus test that I did not do excellent on this morning or the race that I could not win yesterday. It is in that moment of emotional vulnerability and nakedness that I realized, that which prevents me from being perfect is not my inadequacy, but simply definitions of my character. Those things are not haunting sixteenth notes creating anxiety and disappointment, but are just a part of the beautiful music of my life.

As you reflect on your life thus far, what has someone said, written or expressed in some fashion that is especially meaningful to you? Why?

I thought that I was in the wrong place that Thursday night. In reality I could not have been in any better place that night. My church’s substance abuse ministry meeting hosted a weekly outreach meeting for drug addicts and their co-dependents alike. Many were there to try and understand and if fortunate enough, cure a problem that plagued them all. I was there at my pastor’s request to share my experiences with this group. I could relate to the people present at this meeting. My mother had been addicted to drugs for nearly 12 years and is still today. I had to accept the fact that my mother was often missing. Initially it was difficult for me to comprehend why my mother would leave for months or even a year at a time. I pleaded to God nightly to “send my mommy back home”, so often in fact, that I unconsciously said it even when she was at home. My grandmother who raised me through her absence soon gained guardianship over my brothers and me. It was not long before I realized that I was better off without her. I could not survive the abusiveness and lewdness of all of her ‘boyfriends’. I overwhelmingly preferred life with my grandmother. Living with my grandmother was the only way that I could find stability, knowing that I would always have someone to pay attention to me, keep me safe, and prepare dinner every night. My grandmother’s death also meant that I was without a home, as my mother was missing at the time of her death. No one would take on the ‘burden’ of the three of us. Fortunately my living grandmother decided that she would take us in, and we have lived with her since.

How? At that point I wanted to interrupt and tell everyone in that circle exactly what steps I had taken to live despite the poisonous circumstances given. I wanted nothing more than to tell everyone that he/she is not confined to the path that his/her life had molded thus far. I don’t pretend that my past didn’t happen or didn’t affect me.
