greatest things that has come out of this project for me is a renewed love for
Stevie Wonder. Listening to his albums inevitably jogs memories of eight year
old Cam singing the shit out of these songs before puberty hit while not knowing
any of the lyrics because the internet wasn't really a thing yet. I think about
the first time I actually got to see Stevie which was years after my first
attempt to see him landed me in the ER, but thats a story for another day. I
think of Stevie singing at MJs funeral and fucking us all up dearly. I landed in
New York today and just played "Living for the City" as I crossed the bridge and
I felt like I was in a movie.

23 - John Lennon/Plastic Ono Band by John Lennon / Plastic Ono Band
This album is garbage. I'm not surprised I've never followed Lennon's solo
career much because he is basically just Bob Dylan without the Beatles. A lot of
this music is just repetitive and basic and the lyrics almost sound like
Scientology level pseudo intellectualism. This album was not well received when
it was released, Rolling Stone's critics were one of the only ones to think it
was any good and unsurprisingly it's ranked high on a list on which it doesn't
belong. Gimme that 39 minutes of my life back.

22 - The Complete Recordings by Robert Johnson
By my count this is the 11th compilation album on this list and its very long
with pretty poor recording quality, but I actually believe this is probably the
only one that I could accept on this list. Wikipedia says it best: "While Robert
Johnson's professional recording career can be measured in months, his musical
legacy has survived more than 70 years". The great blues artists of the time
were influenced by Robert Johnson and this compilation includes his entire
recording history. This has undeniable historically significant and lays musical
foundations that would last for decades to come. One unfortunate realization
that I had in listening to this is that while this list is pretty significantly
skewed towards celebrating white musicians, African American artists account for
8 of the 11 greatest hits albums I've listened to thus far. In my opinion that
just shows how white this list would have skewed had they not just started
looking for greatest hits albums made by black artists to try and balance it
out.

21 - The Great Twenty_Eight by Chuck Berry
Make that 12 compilations, 9 by African Americans. I didn't want to make this
list so much about race when I started it, I promise, and I stuck with this list
despite its shortcomings because I wanted to explore music that was less
familiar to me. However, in the process an unfortunate narrative has emerged of
black artists not gaining the same critical acclaim that their arguably less
talented white counterparts got. I guess we'll just have to wait and see who the
aliens out there starting to receive radio signals from earth think is better
Bob Dylan or Stevie Wonder. But on subject of this album: Chuck Berry absolutely
rules. It's very clear just how much he influenced rock and roll, at no point
more clear than when I realized Come Together sampled one of its best lines from
"You Can't Catch Me". Also Chuck Berry's infamous riff has got to be the most
signature musical calling card of all time up there with MJs "Hee Hee!" Screech

20 - Thriller by Michael Jackson
Look, I hope you didn't need this list to tell you that this is possibly the
best album ever made (although I would argue Off the Wall is MJs best). I also
hope you know that there is literally no way there are 19 albums better than
this. Beat It, Billie Jean, Wanna Be Starting Something, PYT, Human Nature... I
mean the track-list reads like a greatest hits album. It's the second highest
selling album of all time and the only album above it is a greatest hits album,
so its basically the highest selling album ever so it passes the $$$ test, it
definitely passes the ear test. I politely request that Rolling Stone explain
their rationale for including this album at 20.

19 - Astral Weeks by Van Morrison
I believe the last Van Morrison album I encountered I said was really good but
definitely not better than Off the Wall which was an album or two before. My
reasoning stands here. This album is great, and in a lot of ways gives you
glimpse into the sixties in a way that few albums do. The musicianship on this
album is standout, particularly because he involved a lot of actual jazz
musicians. The album is very spontaneous and experimental but not in a contrived
Yoko Ono way. The ranking of this album here still seems pretty absurd, but I
won't blame Van Morrison for Rolling Stone ranking this too high, I enjoyed it.

18 - Born to Run by Bruce Springsteen
The last Springsteen album was better than this. This album just didn't do
anything for me. I would argue this doesn't even belong in the top 100.

17 - Nevermind by Nirvana
This album is pretty good, but a few really good tracks does not an all-time
great album make. I could justify this album in the top 50 but never in the top
20. The songwriting on this album is pretty basic, and its driven only by the
extremely proficient instrumentation. Apparently, lyrics and singing were
basically saved for the very end when making this album and its pretty apparent
in songs like "Smells Like Teen Spirit" which features basic lyrics mumbled in a
way that makes them barely decipherable.

16 - Blood on the Tracks by Bob Dylan
Quite the opposite of the preceding album, this album features some remarkable
lyrics and more singing than I am used to hearing on a Dylan album. It's crazy
that this was his 15th studio album. All that considered, putting this album in
the top 20 greatest albums is equivalent to giving the Best Picture Oscar to a
RomCom. Yeah you can make great songs if you sound like Bob Dylan when you sing,
but should we really compare that to "real" singers?

15 - Are You Experienced by The Jimi Hendrix Experience
I listened to this album three times before preceding to the next album. That
should tell you something about this album. Everything about this album is
amazing: vocals, instruments, lyrics... a total package. This album starts with
one of the best Hendrix songs and ends on one of the best finishing lines on an
album: "Cuz if my baby don't love me no mo... I know her sister will".

14 - Abbey Road by The Beatles
Ending the Hendrix album on that line and then picking this album up with Come
Together really makes it sound like these albums were meant to be played
sequentially. I already stated earlier in this list that Come Together is the
best song to open an album of all-time and I stand by that (although I amended
my previous list to include Purple Haze which I left off). I also believe this
happens to be the best Beatle's album, and I Want You (She's so Heavy) is my
favorite Beatle's song.

13 - The Velvet Underground & Nico by The Velvet Underground
I'm quite surprised that this late in the list I am still encountering
existential questions about the criteria of what makes an album great. I would
say that the songs on this album are actually quite good, and the album was
definitely ahead of its time, which is why it got no real critical acclaim when
it was made. It's a great example of a piece of art that needs time to be
appreciated. However, there is one thing that I can't get over: the quality of
this recording is fucking AWFUL. This is similar to the watching videos of 90s
basketball. As a kid I was like man this shit is awesome, but now that we live
in a world in HD I can't tell Jordan from Kukoc on the old footage. Some will
make the argument that artists focused on mono recording and all that stuff, but
honestly it sounds like they are playing on shitty instruments too. This
basically sounds like the world's dopest garage band, which still leaves me with
a conundrum about whether this belongs this high on the list. I can only imagine
what this would have sounded like if a professional had been involved in
producing this.

12 - Kind of Blue by Miles Davis
For most of my life before college there were few things I cared more about than
playing jazz trumpet. I distinctly recall beginning to comprehend jazz
specifically because of the song So What on this album. When given the
opportunity to solo on this song I found for the first time that jazz was not
about hitting crazy high notes and doing things that other people can't do.
Miles Davis doesn't really do much in that solo that most trumpet players can't
do, but its the way that he does it, coupled with the absolute simplicity of the
arrangement that conspires to create something genius. I am not alone in
considering this the best jazz album of all time, Rolling Stone and I have at
least found something we can agree on.

11 - The Sun Sessions by Elvis Presley
This album has pretty big historical significance, I'm aware. It's an early look
at what would wind up being on of the most successful music careers of all time.
That doesn't mean that Elvis playing solo in a studio acoustic should be given
greater importance than the many amazing albums it is ranked ahead of which
included much more production quality and performance. This is a compilation
album, but I can actually forgive that in this context since these Sun Studios
sessions was the birth of his career.

10 - The Beatles ("The White Album") by The Beatles
I'm not gonna lie, before starting this list I would have told you this album
was appropriately ranked here, but now... I don't know. Is it a good album? Of
course. But I think all in all this album is too long and much of its content
I'm quite indifferent too. The band was falling apart at this time and I think
that it lacks the togetherness of earlier albums. Also there is an air of
pretentiousness that is pretty palpable, most people would probably blame that
on Lennon and Yoko Ono. All told I would downgrade this album pretty heavily.
It's definitely not better than Abbey Road IMO.

9 - Blonde on Blonde by Bob Dylan
This is one of the most musical albums that Dylan ever made and he actually
sounds like he's a singer in many of these songs, which is refreshing. I have a
hard time believing there is a Dylan album better than this.

8 - London Calling by The Clash
The last Clash album was surprisingly good to me and this one definitely builds
on that. These guys are excellent musicians, and sneaky good singers. The songs
lyrics add a lot to the music here as well, as I've noticed a lot of Rock bands
just don't take lyricism seriously which makes me like this even more. I'm not
saying that I'm all of a sudden a punk rock fan, but I definitely don't hate it
anymore.

7 - Exile on Main St. by The Rolling Stones
I was having dinner with a friend recently who was telling me about a book he's
reading, Keith Richards Memoir. In it they describe how a lot of their music was
made in crazy drug fueled benders so insane that they would take acid to come
down. As someone who has DEFINITELY NEVER DONE ACID this sounded insane to me.
But then listening to this album I thought, how else could this music be made?
There's a certain frenetic and jittery quality to this music that makes it sound
like all of the musicians are in different time zones playing together on a
video conference. All of that said, this album is pretty defining Rock. It would
also not be inaccurate to call this a blues album. Personally, I love Shake your
Hips a lot.

6 - What's Going On by Marvin Gaye
Bold claim: What's going on and blonde are basically the same album. Of all the
albums that I've listened to on this list this album paints a picture clearer
than just about any other. Like Blonde, this album is a true reflection of its
time. While Blonde tells a coming of age story that reflects the values of the
current generation like openness about the spectrum of human sexuality and the
costs of consumerism, What's Going On is the story of the generation that
birthed ours, and is the dawn of an awakening on issues such as climate change,
warfare and inequality. In many ways, these albums are different chapters of the
same book. The parallels are pretty uncanny, as both artists use different
characters who are both similar and dissimilar from themselves to tell their
stories. I could spend all day dissecting these two albums, but fortunately
somebody else has already done that so I'll just say that I think that Marvin
Gaye was reincarnated in Frank Ocean.

5 - Rubber Soul by The Beatles
I didn't realize I had been sleeping on this album. The Beatles make you feel
like no band had ever sang and harmonized together before them. Songs like You
Won't See me are so remarkable simple and corny but just work so well. I know
literally anyone can sing oooo la la la ooooo la la la, but there's something
about the Beatles. This album marked the introduction of new instruments for the
band like the Sitar, and laid the ground-work for a lot of their later work. I
think in a lot of ways this is the purest Beatles album as it touches on all of
the characteristics that defined the band over the course of their career. It's
also worth noting that You Won't See me is the ONLY song on this album over 3
minutes long which sorta defines this album, a series of very good songs that
are only as long as they need to be.

4 - Highway 61 Revisited by Bob Dylan
It's very difficult to pick the better Dylan album out of this and Blonde on
Blonde. This album is very classic in its construction and execution, and it has
some really amazing high points like "Ballad of a Thin Man", probably my
favorite Dylan song. It reminds me of Tarantino movie. I think being
significantly shorter than Blonde on Blonde it left me with fewer memorable
moments.

3 - Revolver by The Beatles
Taxman fucking slaps. What a way to start off an album. This album is so
psychedelic and diverse in its music, with rare low points. This album must be
appreciated on a very good set of speakers or headphones. The string octet, the
harmonies all add up to what feels like it must be longer than the 35 minutes it
actually clocks in at. There definitely not a bad song on this album. This album
is basically an advertisement for LSD, and a good one. Also "Got To Get You Into
My Life" is one of my favorite Beatles songs, and is in the running for one of
the best Beatles covers by Earth Wind and Fire.

2 - Pet Sounds by The Beach Boys
The Beach Boys have such amazingly complex harmonies, similar to Phish, but I
have no interest in making that comparison. I think it says something that Acid
was pretty influential on the Top 3 albums on this list. The relationship
between music and LSD is storied and well documented, but there are cleary some
things that came out of psychedelic rock that rarely appears in other genres
save Jazz (and this album is jazz at points). Pet Sounds just brings together
some of the coolest --for lack of a better word-- sounds. The two instrumental
tracks on this album are both really amazing and really ties the album together
in a magnificent way. "God Only Knows" is such a triumphant song that was
endowed an even more special place in my heart by being on the Love Actually
Soundtrack. I think that this is the best album on this list and determining
which album is better between this and Blonde would require intensive study and
could potentially take the rest of my life. I think comparing the Mono and the
Stereo versions on this album is a very fun undertaking.

1 - Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band by The Beatles
Ok, everyone knows this album rules. "With a Little Help From My Friends" is
easily one of the best Beatles songs. It also has one of the greatest covers of
all time thanks to the man Joe Cocker. Obviously, "Lucy In The Sky With
Diamonds" weird, cool, love it. I personally love "She's Leaving Home" a song
that I learned to waltz to. I've heard this album many times, and it never gets
tired. I think that familiarity can work against an album sometimes, not here.
This album like Revolver is just jam packed with great songs with no particular
weak points. It's an incredibly close call, but I think I prefer Revolver by a
hair.

Conclusion
I think that at the end of the day there are tiers of album excellence:

First
These albums are nearly perfect compositions for their societal impact, musical
mastery and creativity.

 * Pet Sounds
 * Revolver
 * Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band
 * What's Going On
 * Rubber Soul
 * Kind of Blue
 * Whitney Houston
 * Are You Experienced
 * Thriller
 * Blonde on Blonde
 * Off the Wall
 * Innervisions
 * Dark Side of the Moon
 * The Miseducation of Lauryn Hill
 * Rumours
 * Tapestry
 * Hotel California
 * Legend
 * My Beautiful Dark Twisted Fantasy
 * Speakerboxx/Love Below
 * Innervisions
 * Upsrising
 * Purple Rain
 * A Love Supreme

Third
 * Awaken My Love
 * Still Bill
 * Kid A
 * Aja
 * The Wall
 * Zepplin 4
 * Moanin
 * Controversy
 * Damn
 * Random Access Memories
 * Time Out
 * A Charlie Brown Christmas

{"version":"0.3.1","markups":[],"atoms":[],"cards":[["markdown",{"cardName":"card-markdown","markdown":"
### Who Am I?

I have been playing fantasy sports continuously for 7-8 years now and have managed just about every league I've played in. I would imagine that is far older the majority of leagues today^[I could not  find exact statistics on this readily available online but imagine the best source on this subject is the Fantasy Sports Trade Association, which I'll refer to throughout these footnotes as the FSTA. They produce an [industry demographics report](https://fsta.org/research/industry-demographics/) that appears to be updated regularly.]. That said, one league that I play in managed by the dad of one of my close friends has been playing since the scores were calculated by hand from newspaper box scores in the newspaper^[According to [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fantasy_sport#Early_history), the concept of fantasy sports has been around since WWII, and the first fantasy football league came about in 1962], so I'm not claiming to be any sort of expert on the matter. 

### Industry Background

Daily Fantasy Sports have played a particularly oversized role in the immense growth of the total industry. ^[These stats are from 2015 but based on the FSTA data ![Fantasy-Infographic_V3-2](__GHOST_URL__/content/images/2018/01/Fantasy-Infographic_V3-2.jpg)]

http://mailchi.mp/fsta/fsta-summer-conference-less-than-3-weeks-remaining-1739733
"}]],"sections":[[10,0]],"ghostVersion":"3.0"}
posts.html:
Who Am I?

I have been playing fantasy sports continuously for 7-8 years now and have managed just about every league I've played in. I would imagine that is far older the majority of leagues today[1]. That said, one league that I play in managed by the dad of one of my close friends has been playing since the scores were calculated by hand from newspaper box scores in the newspaper[2], so I'm not claiming to be any sort of expert on the matter.

Industry Background

Daily Fantasy Sports have played a particularly oversized role in the immense growth of the total industry. [3]

Who Am I?
I have been playing fantasy sports continuously for 7-8 years now and have
managed just about every league I've played in. I would imagine that is far
older the majority of leagues today[1]. That said, one league that I play in
managed by the dad of one of my close friends has been playing since the scores
were calculated by hand from newspaper box scores in the newspaper[2], so I'm
not claiming to be any sort of expert on the matter.

Industry Background
Daily Fantasy Sports have played a particularly oversized role in the immense
growth of the total industry. [3]

http://mailchi.mp/fsta/fsta-summer-conference-less-than-3-weeks-remaining-1739733


--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 1. I could not find exact statistics on this readily available online but
    imagine the best source on this subject is the Fantasy Sports Trade
    Association, which I'll refer to throughout these footnotes as the FSTA.
    They produce an industry demographics report
    [https://fsta.org/research/industry-demographics/] that appears to be
    updated regularly. ↩︎
    
    
 2. According to Wikipedia
    [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fantasy_sport#Early_history], the concept of
    fantasy sports has been around since WWII, and the first fantasy football
    league came about in 1962 ↩︎
    
    
 3. These stats are from 2015 but based on the FSTA data ↩︎

posts.title: Move fast and steal things
posts.mobiledoc:
{"version":"0.3.1","atoms":[],"cards":[["markdown",{"cardName":"card-markdown","markdown":"Yesterday I stumbled across an [article](https://www.revealnews.org/article/facebook-knowingly-duped-game-playing-kids-and-their-parents-out-of-money/) that absolutely disgusted me. I would seriously recommend reading the entire thing but the gist of the story is that for a period beginning in 2010 --and potentially ongoing-- Facebook willfully defrauded it's users by using opaque techniques to trick minors into charging large amounts to their parents credit card and subsequently refusing to refund the charges because it would have negatively affect their revenue. Internally, the referred to these children as whales... Yes, a damn casino phrase.

Unlike Las Vegas, which was founded by mobsters and is well know for it's inherent sleaziness and low moral fortitude, Silicon Valley is a place that tries hard to create the impression that it is building products that make the world a better place and doing it in as responsible and ethical a way as possible. But stories like this, Russia Collusion, Fyre festival, Theranos, Pentagon contracts, censored search engines and the like have made it very clear that we are a long way from "Don't Be Evil". A cursory Google search will show you that this is something that pretty much everyone agrees is a rampant issue, and a potentially fatal flaw for the industry.

As the engineering manager at Burrow, It became important for me to try and understand the problem, it's causes and ponder it's solutions. My company recently concluded the process of adding an additional engineer to put team, a process that took much longer than we anticipated. In the many interviews that I conducted in this process when asked by potential hires what was good about working at Burrow I often responded that we do things "the right way". In regards to writing code that means we don't cut corners for poor solutions that will come back to haunt us. But also we make our couches using sustainably sourced materials that don't contain harmful materials. We even make them in America which reduces the carbon footprint and created jobs in our communities. All of these things do not help our bottom line, but helps us sleep at night.

I'm self-aware enough to realize that a lot of these companies probably said the same things about how they operated when they were a private company with fewer than 50 employees. One obvious explanation for the total lack of business ethics in tech (which I may refer to interchangably as Silicon Valley despite the fact that my company is located in NYC) is that there is simply no such thing. Businesses, especially publicly traded companies have no obligation to ethics, only profit. While Facebook may have started with the altruistic motivation to connect people in the world that was far easier when you're spending VC money and focused on growth and product. As they grew to become a business in the traditional sense it became harder and harder for their ethical compass to find north. 

Juul: the path to hell is paved with great intentions.

business ethics and corporate morality don't exist, because they are inherently antithetical

"}]],"markups":[],"sections":[[10,0]],"ghostVersion":"3.0"}
posts.html:
Yesterday I stumbled across an article that absolutely disgusted me. I would seriously recommend reading the entire thing but the gist of the story is that for a period beginning in 2010 --and potentially ongoing-- Facebook willfully defrauded it's users by using opaque techniques to trick minors into charging large amounts to their parents credit card and subsequently refusing to refund the charges because it would have negatively affect their revenue. Internally, the referred to these children as whales... Yes, a damn casino phrase.

Unlike Las Vegas, which was founded by mobsters and is well know for it's inherent sleaziness and low moral fortitude, Silicon Valley is a place that tries hard to create the impression that it is building products that make the world a better place and doing it in as responsible and ethical a way as possible. But stories like this, Russia Collusion, Fyre festival, Theranos, Pentagon contracts, censored search engines and the like have made it very clear that we are a long way from "Don't Be Evil". A cursory Google search will show you that this is something that pretty much everyone agrees is a rampant issue, and a potentially fatal flaw for the industry.

As the engineering manager at Burrow, It became important for me to try and understand the problem, it's causes and ponder it's solutions. My company recently concluded the process of adding an additional engineer to put team, a process that took much longer than we anticipated. In the many interviews that I conducted in this process when asked by potential hires what was good about working at Burrow I often responded that we do things "the right way". In regards to writing code that means we don't cut corners for poor solutions that will come back to haunt us. But also we make our couches using sustainably sourced materials that don't contain harmful materials. We even make them in America which reduces the carbon footprint and created jobs in our communities. All of these things do not help our bottom line, but helps us sleep at night.

I'm self-aware enough to realize that a lot of these companies probably said the same things about how they operated when they were a private company with fewer than 50 employees. One obvious explanation for the total lack of business ethics in tech (which I may refer to interchangably as Silicon Valley despite the fact that my company is located in NYC) is that there is simply no such thing. Businesses, especially publicly traded companies have no obligation to ethics, only profit. While Facebook may have started with the altruistic motivation to connect people in the world that was far easier when you're spending VC money and focused on growth and product. As they grew to become a business in the traditional sense it became harder and harder for their ethical compass to find north.

Juul: the path to hell is paved with great intentions.

business ethics and corporate morality don't exist, because they are inherently antithetical
posts.plaintext:
Yesterday I stumbled across an article
[https://www.revealnews.org/article/facebook-knowingly-duped-game-playing-kids-and-their-parents-out-of-money/] 
that absolutely disgusted me. I would seriously recommend reading the entire
thing but the gist of the story is that for a period beginning in 2010 --and
potentially ongoing-- Facebook willfully defrauded it's users by using opaque
techniques to trick minors into charging large amounts to their parents credit
card and subsequently refusing to refund the charges because it would have
negatively affect their revenue. Internally, the referred to these children as
whales... Yes, a damn casino phrase.

Unlike Las Vegas, which was founded by mobsters and is well know for it's
inherent sleaziness and low moral fortitude, Silicon Valley is a place that
tries hard to create the impression that it is building products that make the
world a better place and doing it in as responsible and ethical a way as
possible. But stories like this, Russia Collusion, Fyre festival, Theranos,
Pentagon contracts, censored search engines and the like have made it very clear
that we are a long way from "Don't Be Evil". A cursory Google search will show
you that this is something that pretty much everyone agrees is a rampant issue,
and a potentially fatal flaw for the industry.

As the engineering manager at Burrow, It became important for me to try and
understand the problem, it's causes and ponder it's solutions. My company
recently concluded the process of adding an additional engineer to put team, a
process that took much longer than we anticipated. In the many interviews that I
conducted in this process when asked by potential hires what was good about
working at Burrow I often responded that we do things "the right way". In
regards to writing code that means we don't cut corners for poor solutions that
will come back to haunt us. But also we make our couches using sustainably
sourced materials that don't contain harmful materials. We even make them in
America which reduces the carbon footprint and created jobs in our communities.
All of these things do not help our bottom line, but helps us sleep at night.

I'm self-aware enough to realize that a lot of these companies probably said the
same things about how they operated when they were a private company with fewer
than 50 employees. One obvious explanation for the total lack of business ethics
in tech (which I may refer to interchangably as Silicon Valley despite the fact
that my company is located in NYC) is that there is simply no such thing.
Businesses, especially publicly traded companies have no obligation to ethics,
only profit. While Facebook may have started with the altruistic motivation to
connect people in the world that was far easier when you're spending VC money
and focused on growth and product. As they grew to become a business in the
traditional sense it became harder and harder for their ethical compass to find
north.

Juul: the path to hell is paved with great intentions.

business ethics and corporate morality don't exist, because they are inherently
antithetical

posts.html:
Rekordbox

This is really the gold standard of DJing

  • Format a thumb drive (Use FAT 32 format)
  • Start importing all of your music to iTunes
  • Collect high quality sounds (Greater than 256 Bitrate)
  • Sync Library between iTunes and Rekordbox

There needs to be some improvement in the space

Rekordbox app is a great tool

That sort of self-reflection is often elucidating, but busy people are known to skip those steps. When taking inbound requests don't just settle for the what. Make sure that the problem is stated, if there is a proposed solution its detailed and the expected impact is made clear. There are many different opinions on how to handle prioritization and I'll leave that to another post but some useful concepts that I have found for prioritization are "],[0,[4],1,"70/20/10 time"],[0,[],0," and "],[0,[5],1,"#now #next #later"],[0,[],0,"."]]],[1,"p",[[0,[],0,"Bug databases are also a very polarizing topic for PMs. While everyone agrees they are necessary, sometimes they just become a dumping ground for things that are more likely to become invalid before they are fixed. When I do my weekly or bi-weekly backlog grooming I am always looking for ways to tag issues to more easily find related issues. That way whenever I am deciding to work on something in a specific sprint I always check to see if there is anything else in the backlog that's related that it may make sense to knock out alongside the stuff that we are planning to address. "],[0,[6],1,"Not all bugs are created equal."],[0,[],0," Every company should have a tag or "],[0,[7],1,"duress code"],[0,[],0," that means \"the sky is falling, drop whatever you are working on and fix this right now\"."]]],[1,"h3",[[0,[],0,"Working with Engineers"]]],[1,"p",[[0,[],0,"Obviously this was an area where I constantly had a lot of opinions and questions. In many ways I felt like a wolf in sheep's clothing because while PMs have a lot of authority as I stated earlier in most cases it actually falls on the engineers to actually \"get the thing done\" and their proximity to the actual product endows a lot of opinions and authority. Knowing how to avoid getting in circular arguments or \"pissing contests\" with engineers is a life skill for anyone who works in tech, and any PM who hopes to be successful must obviously cultivate great relationships with their engineers. In my experience, a quality that is shared by all engineers is curiosity. With that in mind you can often engage them on much better terms with the right question, rather than only providing answers."]]],[1,"p",[[0,[],0,"Also as a PM there may be many things on your roadmap, but you cannot allow that to make you blind to technical debt. Tech debt affects engineering productivity, and so while it may not be the sexiest thing to make time for, it  cannot be ignored. There were many good suggestions about how to handle tech debt, but I think that a good rule of thumb is that you should dedicate some percentage of each sprint (~20%) to tech debt. Some teams even rotate one engineer a sprint to work on tech debt. However, you go about it you should build tech debt into your planning or it won't happen."]]],[1,"p",[[0,[],0,"One way that teams consistently fail to get the most out of their engineers is by not involving them in the design process until the designs are final. "],[0,[6],1,"This is the single biggest missed opportunity I've seen in my career."],[0,[],0," Engineers should be consulted early and often throughout the product process because their knowledge of technical constraints and requirements are essential knowledge that no one else on the team is likely to have. Without the input of an engineer any design you create has a high possibility of being far more difficult than you think (or nearly impossible) or more often incomplete."]]],[1,"p",[[0,[],0,"In working with engineers there will inevitably be times where engineers just say no. Its important to remember that no is the beginning of a conversation, not the end. There are many reasons why someone might be saying no, Maybe the vision isn't contagious enough, maybe they're just not good enough to execute it. But any engineer who uses no as a brick wall should be a red flag. A good engineer is going to find ways to say no without saying no, which moves the conversation forward."]]],[1,"p",[[0,[],0,"Above all else, build meaningful relationships with every engineer you work with. Engineers can be productivity boosters and at times outright miracle workers. I have a common refrain I make within my company when asked if we can do X for the website: \"Anything is possible, it's just a matter of how\". Engineers want to solve your problems, solving problems is why we love our job. You just have to be willing to work with us to make sure we understand what you're asking and listen to our opinions about how to accomplish the solution. I could go on on this topic for days, but that's for another post."]]],[1,"h3",[[0,[],0,"Working with Designers"]]],[1,"p",[[0,[],0,"When I first started working in Tech I was working as a designer. One of the main reasons that I transitioned to engineering was because I was working in companies with limited PMs and as the designer I had to advocate myself directly to engineers to get the things I was designing implemented. This was a very difficult process, and while good interpersonal skills allowed me to build good relationships and be successful at times, I more often found myself shut down by an engineer with very little recourse to advocate for my ideas. A good product manager is capable of paying "],[0,[8],1,"as close attention to the details as a good designer"],[0,[],0," and advocating for their very detailed vision. Non-designers will often want to make what they think are small tweaks to designs because they don't understand the minutiae that makes the experience the designer is aiming for."]]],[1,"p",[[0,[],0,"Designers can be a very scarce resource no matter what size your company is, and there is often a long line of team members wanting to borrow designers to work on their needs. While this can be unavoidable pairing designers with people who don't understand design (and there are plenty people who think they do who I'm just gonna say it "],[0,[6],1,"DON'T"],[0,[],0,") can be harmful to the designers experience and growth. Designers need to work with other designers the same way engineers need to work with engineers to get better. Treating your design team as an internal agency of design contractors is not the way to build the best design team. This is where PMs can seriously affect the quality of life of designers. Similar to engineers, designers have a lot of understanding of the actual constraints and intricacies of the product and need PMs to translate their knowledge for those that don't have it."]]],[1,"p",[[0,[],0,"With all of this in mind, you should be involving your designers and engineers very early on in the product process. Some companies fall into the pattern of just throwing things over the wall to other departments and expecting that they can just take it and \"do their thing\" from there.  Throwing things over the wall can be a very dangerous process, you can't insure that the people you're tossing it to will catch it."]]],[1,"h3",[[0,[],0,"Final Thoughts"]]],[1,"p",[[0,[],0,"An analogy that I often use when I think of PMs is that their role is really similar to that of cartilage or other connect tissue in the body. They are there to make enable movement, but like cartilage are there to provide some cushion to avoid very harmful bone on bone action. PMs fill "],[0,[9],1,"in many gaps "],[0,[],0,"and can be called upon to do all sorts of different tasks because frankly, no one else can or will. In basketball, one might call this position the garbage man, those guys on the floor who may not be getting plays called for them, but are out there always doing what needs to be done, including the dirty work. They are motivators, hype men and enforcers if they have to be. When these guys make a play it can spark their entire a team to go on a run. It's an imperfect analogy because of the authority that PMs possess in an organization, but I think a good PM should have a garbage man mentality."]]],[10,0],[1,"h2",[[0,[],0,"Interview Prep"]]],[3,"ul",[[[0,[10],1,"https://hackernoon.com/how-to-prepare-for-a-product-manager-interview-6204b1ba5d6d#.susfkrtvl"]],[[0,[11],1,"https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/20141020034027-1533734-acing-the-product-management-interview/"]],[[0,[12],1,"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CshJxTcsgvo"]]]],[1,"h2",[[0,[],0,"Further Reading"]]],[3,"ul",[[[0,[13],1,"https://medium.com/@noah_weiss/50-articles-and-books-that-will-make-you-a-great-product-manager-aad5babee2f7"]],[[0,[14],1,"https://medium.com/product-manager-club/10-ways-to-ruin-your-product-98bec44877da"]],[[0,[15],1,"https://medium.com/the-year-of-the-looking-glass/how-to-work-with-engineers-a3163ff1eced?"]],[[0,[16],1,"https://medium.com/the-year-of-the-looking-glass/how-to-work-with-pms-3e852d5eccf5"]],[[0,[9],1,"https://medium.com/startup-grind/a-map-of-white-space-for-product-managers-17d65c397749"]],[[0,[17],1,"https://medium.com/the-year-of-the-looking-glass/how-to-work-with-designers-6c975dede146"]],[[0,[18],1,"https://www.kennorton.com/essays/how-to-work-with-software-engineers.html"]],[[0,[19],1,"https://blackboxofpm.com/mvpm-minimum-viable-product-manager-e1aeb8dd421"]],[[0,[20],1,"Good Product Manager Bad Product Manager"]],[[0,[21],1,"A Hitchhikers Guide to Product Management"]],[[0,[14],1,"https://medium.com/product-manager-club/10-ways-to-ruin-your-product-98bec44877da"]],[[0,[22],1,"https://medium.com/startup-grind/a-map-of-white-space-for-product-managers-17d65c397749?"]],[[0,[23],1,"https://blackboxofpm.com/"]]]],[1,"p",[]]],"ghostVersion":"3.0"}
posts.html:
Last fall I attended the NEA product summit in San Francisco. The day was jam packed with a lot of extremely informative and inspiring conversations. I learned many things from product leaders from many companies in tech from Notion to Casper, and have since put a lot of the things I've learned into action at my company, Burrow. As one of the few engineers/engineering managers present, I felt that I had a very unique perspective, and took away insights that are especially helpful for me. Since Burrow is a very small team my role is not only to lead the engineering team, but to take on significant product management responsibilities so I was eager to learn every tip I could to become a better PM, while drawing on my expertise as an engineer. These are my stories:

The day kicked off with what would wind up being the most engaging and informative of all the sessions. Product leaders from a half dozen different organizations formed a panel to discuss how they go about building high performance product teams with attention to the skill-sets that they seek as well as the org structure that they aim for. This conversation actually could have gone on all day, and should likely be the singular theme for a future summit/conference. All of the companies represented were made up slightly differently, but there were some key takeaways that most present agreed on. Most of the points I'll go over here come from that conversation, but I will sprinkle in learnings from other more specific talks from the summit.

Team Structure

What is the perfect ratio of engineers, designers and product managers? It is an age old question with no hard-fast answer. This may seem like a cop out, but the answer depends on your business. One thing is clear, you should organize your teams for what you are trying to optimize for and you should revisit this organization at least yearly. I believe that cross-functional teams have all sorts of advantages that you don't get when you are splitting your teams by function, but also requires that your team members have more knowledge of the other roles on their team. Some companies use trios of Eng/Design/PM, some use pods, others use squads. In fact, one company at the summit doesn't employ any product managers at all, a fact that was met with gasps from some in the crowd. Whatever structure you decide on make sure that you are making that choice based on what you are trying to accomplish with the team, not just because you read a blog post about it. In my opinion, you should definitely build cross functional teams. I have been in organizations that split their teams up based on function and often times it winds up creating multiple echo chambers.

Don't Hire Too Many PMs

It was said and repeated several times that one of the worst things you can do to a team is to add too many PMs to it. Everyone present understood that as the champions for the product PMs are absolutely essential to a team doing well, but when you add to many PMs to the mix things can go south in many ways. It is especially difficult to keep PMs motivated if they are making plans that don't happen or are in consistently in conflict with other PMs for resources.

“Too much of anything is bad, but too much good whiskey is barely enough.” - Mark Twain

Disagree and Commit

One of the phrases that consistently came up and really resonated with everyone present was the idea that in good product teams there is a time to debate, and a time to put the debate aside and move forward. As the champions of the product and the owner of the roadmap a PM inherently possess a lot of authority. If one wields that authority incorrectly though, it doesn't matter. The most productive way to move forward when you find yourself at odds with others in an organization is to simply say "I hear what you're saying, and I respect your opinion, but I simply don't agree. So let's note this disagreement and follow up on this later, but for right now we have to commit to this course of action." A good PM is not going to flaunt their authority, but it essential that they use it tactfully to keep things moving along.

Career Advancement

It's true for any hiring, but without a clear path for career advancement it is difficult to maintain the best people. I don't want to get too much into specifics here because what's right for one company or employee could be totally wrong for another, but one critical takeaway I gathered on the subject was that you have to always make sure that you have parallel career tracks for individual contributors (IC) and management. For example, the assumption that your best engineers will make your best managers is quite often not the case, so ICs should not be stuck in their advancement if management is not a good fit for them.

Respect everyone's time

One thing that almost everyone present could relate to was how at various times in their org's history people's time was being wasted for various reasons. While this is a general issue across all areas of any business, this problem is especially impactful for product teams. Everyone can acknowledge that face-to-face time really matters especially in a cross-functional setting, but meetings also come at a cost. I heard horror stories of teams rolling into meetings 40 people deep and mostly wasting everyone's time. Early on in my company we had a company wide daily standup that I absolutely detested because 1. it was 7am in my timezone and 2. It was completely useless. Successful product teams understand the Maker's Schedule vs Manager's Schedule and try to strike the right balance when planning meetings. There are ways to mitigate this, for example, building out documentation allows for a lot more asynchronous communication.

Prioritization

Probably one of the most essential responsibilities a PM has is making decisions on how to prioritize what their team works on. While the specifics of how this process should happen vary drastically from team to team, any product manager has to be very good at establishing priority. One way to improve prioritization across a company is to have each team lead constantly prioritizing the things that they are asking of the product team. That sort of self-reflection is often elucidating, but busy people are known to skip those steps. When taking inbound requests don't just settle for the what. Make sure that the problem is stated, if there is a proposed solution its detailed and the expected impact is made clear. There are many different opinions on how to handle prioritization and I'll leave that to another post but some useful concepts that I have found for prioritization are 70/20/10 time and #now #next #later.

Bug databases are also a very polarizing topic for PMs. While everyone agrees they are necessary, sometimes they just become a dumping ground for things that are more likely to become invalid before they are fixed. As an engineer this is an area that I have particularly strong opinions on and what I believe to be a useful prescription. Obviously, no backlog is useful if you don't regularly groom it. When I do my weekly or bi-weekly backlog grooming I am always looking for ways to tag issues to more easily find related issues. That way whenever I am deciding to work on something in a specific sprint I always check to see if there is anything else in the backlog that's related that it may make sense to knock out alongside the stuff that we are planning to address. Not all bugs are created equal. Every company should have a tag or duress code that means "the sky is falling, drop whatever you are working on and fix this right now".

Working with Engineers

Obviously this was an area where I constantly had a lot of opinions and questions. In many ways I felt like a wolf in sheep's clothing because while PMs have a lot of authority as I stated earlier in most cases it actually falls on the engineers to actually "get the thing done" and their proximity to the actual product endows a lot of opinions and authority. Knowing how to avoid getting in circular arguments or "pissing contests" with engineers is a life skill for anyone who works in tech, and any PM who hopes to be successful must obviously cultivate great relationships with their engineers. In my experience, a quality that is shared by all engineers is curiosity. With that in mind you can often engage them on much better terms with the right question, rather than only providing answers.

Also as a PM there may be many things on your roadmap, but you cannot allow that to make you blind to technical debt. Tech debt affects engineering productivity, and so while it may not be the sexiest thing to make time for, it  cannot be ignored. There were many good suggestions about how to handle tech debt, but I think that a good rule of thumb is that you should dedicate some percentage of each sprint (~20%) to tech debt. Some teams even rotate one engineer a sprint to work on tech debt. However, you go about it you should build tech debt into your planning or it won't happen.

One way that teams consistently fail to get the most out of their engineers is by not involving them in the design process until the designs are final. This is the single biggest missed opportunity I've seen in my career. Engineers should be consulted early and often throughout the product process because their knowledge of technical constraints and requirements are essential knowledge that no one else on the team is likely to have. Without the input of an engineer any design you create has a high possibility of being far more difficult than you think (or nearly impossible) or more often incomplete.

In working with engineers there will inevitably be times where engineers just say no. Its important to remember that no is the beginning of a conversation, not the end. There are many reasons why someone might be saying no, Maybe the vision isn't contagious enough, maybe they're just not good enough to execute it. But any engineer who uses no as a brick wall should be a red flag. A good engineer is going to find ways to say no without saying no, which moves the conversation forward.

Above all else, build meaningful relationships with every engineer you work with. Engineers can be productivity boosters and at times outright miracle workers. I have a common refrain I make within my company when asked if we can do X for the website: "Anything is possible, it's just a matter of how". Engineers want to solve your problems, solving problems is why we love our job. You just have to be willing to work with us to make sure we understand what you're asking and listen to our opinions about how to accomplish the solution. I could go on on this topic for days, but that's for another post.

Working with Designers

When I first started working in Tech I was working as a designer. One of the main reasons that I transitioned to engineering was because I was working in companies with limited PMs and as the designer I had to advocate myself directly to engineers to get the things I was designing implemented. This was a very difficult process, and while good interpersonal skills allowed me to build good relationships and be successful at times, I more often found myself shut down by an engineer with very little recourse to advocate for my ideas. A good product manager is capable of paying as close attention to the details as a good designer and advocating for their very detailed vision. Non-designers will often want to make what they think are small tweaks to designs because they don't understand the minutiae that makes the experience the designer is aiming for.

Designers can be a very scarce resource no matter what size your company is, and there is often a long line of team members wanting to borrow designers to work on their needs. While this can be unavoidable pairing designers with people who don't understand design (and there are plenty people who think they do who I'm just gonna say it DON'T) can be harmful to the designers experience and growth. Designers need to work with other designers the same way engineers need to work with engineers to get better. Treating your design team as an internal agency of design contractors is not the way to build the best design team. This is where PMs can seriously affect the quality of life of designers. Similar to engineers, designers have a lot of understanding of the actual constraints and intricacies of the product and need PMs to translate their knowledge for those that don't have it.

With all of this in mind, you should be involving your designers and engineers very early on in the product process. Some companies fall into the pattern of just throwing things over the wall to other departments and expecting that they can just take it and "do their thing" from there.  Throwing things over the wall can be a very dangerous process, you can't insure that the people you're tossing it to will catch it.

Final Thoughts

An analogy that I often use when I think of PMs is that their role is really similar to that of cartilage or other connect tissue in the body. They are there to make enable movement, but like cartilage are there to provide some cushion to avoid very harmful bone on bone action. PMs fill in many gaps and can be called upon to do all sorts of different tasks because frankly, no one else can or will. In basketball, one might call this position the garbage man, those guys on the floor who may not be getting plays called for them, but are out there always doing what needs to be done, including the dirty work. They are motivators, hype men and enforcers if they have to be. When these guys make a play it can spark their entire a team to go on a run. It's an imperfect analogy because of the authority that PMs possess in an organization, but I think a good PM should have a garbage man mentality.

Interview Prep

posts.plaintext:
Last fall I attended the NEA product summit in San Francisco. The day was jam
packed with a lot of extremely informative and inspiring conversations. I
learned many things from product leaders from many companies in tech from Notion
to Casper, and have since put a lot of the things I've learned into action at my
company, Burrow. As one of the few engineers/engineering managers present, I
felt that I had a very unique perspective, and took away insights that are
especially helpful for me. Since Burrow is a very small team my role is not only
to lead the engineering team, but to take on significant product management
responsibilities so I was eager to learn every tip I could to become a better
PM, while drawing on my expertise as an engineer. These are my stories:

The day kicked off with what would wind up being the most engaging and
informative of all the sessions. Product leaders from a half dozen different
organizations formed a panel to discuss how they go about building high
performance product teams with attention to the skill-sets that they seek as
well as the org structure that they aim for. This conversation actually could
have gone on all day, and should likely be the singular theme for a future
summit/conference. All of the companies represented were made up slightly
differently, but there were some key takeaways that most present agreed on. Most
of the points I'll go over here come from that conversation, but I will sprinkle
in learnings from other more specific talks from the summit.

Team Structure
What is the perfect ratio of engineers, designers and product managers? It is an
age old question with no hard-fast answer. This may seem like a cop out, but the
answer depends on your business. One thing is clear, you should organize your
teams for what you are trying to optimize for and you should revisit this
organization at least yearly. I believe that cross-functional teams have all
sorts of advantages that you don't get when you are splitting your teams by
function, but also requires that your team members have more knowledge of the
other roles on their team. Some companies use trios of Eng/Design/PM, some use 
pods [https://blog.walkme.com/pod-structure-in-rnd-teams/], others use squads
[https://highfive.com/blog/highfive-team-engineering-squad-model]. In fact, one
company at the summit doesn't employ any product managers at all, a fact that
was met with gasps from some in the crowd. Whatever structure you decide on make
sure that you are making that choice based on what you are trying to accomplish
with the team, not just because you read a blog post about it. In my opinion,
you should definitely build cross functional teams. I have been in organizations
that split their teams up based on function and often times it winds up creating
multiple echo chambers.

Don't Hire Too Many PMs
It was said and repeated several times that one of the worst things you can do
to a team is to add too many PMs to it. Everyone present understood that as the
champions for the product PMs are absolutely essential to a team doing well, but
when you add to many PMs to the mix things can go south in many ways. It is
especially difficult to keep PMs motivated if they are making plans that don't
happen or are in consistently in conflict with other PMs for resources.

“Too much of anything is bad, but too much good whiskey is barely enough.” -
Mark Twain


Disagree and Commit
One of the phrases that consistently came up and really resonated with everyone
present was the idea that in good product teams there is a time to debate, and a
time to put the debate aside and move forward. As the champions of the product
and the owner of the roadmap a PM inherently possess a lot of authority. If one
wields that authority incorrectly though, it doesn't matter. The most productive
way to move forward when you find yourself at odds with others in an
organization is to simply say "I hear what you're saying, and I respect your
opinion, but I simply don't agree. So let's note this disagreement and follow up
on this later, but for right now we have to commit to this course of action." A
good PM is not going to flaunt their authority, but it essential that they use
it tactfully to keep things moving along.

Career Advancement
It's true for any hiring, but without a clear path for career advancement it is
difficult to maintain the best people. I don't want to get too much into
specifics here because what's right for one company or employee could be totally
wrong for another, but one critical takeaway I gathered on the subject was that
you have to always make sure that you have parallel career tracks for individual
contributors (IC) and management. For example, the assumption that your best
engineers will make your best managers is quite often not the case, so ICs
should not be stuck in their advancement if management is not a good fit for
them.

Respect everyone's time
One thing that almost everyone present could relate to was how at various times
in their org's history people's time was being wasted for various reasons. While
this is a general issue across all areas of any business, this problem is
especially impactful for product teams. Everyone can acknowledge that
face-to-face time really matters especially in a cross-functional setting, but
meetings also come at a cost. I heard horror stories of teams rolling into
meetings 40 people deep and mostly wasting everyone's time. Early on in my
company we had a company wide daily standup that I absolutely detested because
1. it was 7am in my timezone and 2. It was completely useless. Successful
product teams understand the Maker's Schedule vs Manager's Schedule
[http://www.paulgraham.com/makersschedule.html] and try to strike the right
balance when planning meetings. There are ways to mitigate this, for example,
building out documentation allows for a lot more asynchronous communication.

Prioritization
Probably one of the most essential responsibilities a PM has is making decisions
on how to prioritize what their team works on. While the specifics of how this
process should happen vary drastically from team to team, any product manager
has to be very good at establishing priority. One way to improve prioritization
across a company is to have each team lead constantly prioritizing the things
that they are asking of the product team. That sort of self-reflection is often
elucidating, but busy people are known to skip those steps. When taking inbound
requests don't just settle for the what. Make sure that the problem is stated,
if there is a proposed solution its detailed and the expected impact is made
clear. There are many different opinions on how to handle prioritization and
I'll leave that to another post but some useful concepts that I have found for
prioritization are 70/20/10 time
[https://medium.com/pminsider/manage-your-time-like-google-invests-its-resources-70-20-10-3bb4d600abaa?] 
and #now #next #later
[https://medium.com/@noah_weiss/now-next-later-roadmaps-without-the-drudgery-1cfe65656645]
.

Bug databases are also a very polarizing topic for PMs. While everyone agrees
they are necessary, sometimes they just become a dumping ground for things that
are more likely to become invalid before they are fixed. As an engineer this is
an area that I have particularly strong opinions on and what I believe to be a

for ways to tag issues to more easily find related issues. That way whenever I

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Duress_code] that means "the sky is falling, drop
whatever you are working on and fix this right now".

You just have to be willing to work with us to make sure we understand what
you're asking and listen to our opinions about how to accomplish the solution. I
could go on on this topic for days, but that's for another post.

Working with Designers
When I first started working in Tech I was working as a designer. One of the
main reasons that I transitioned to engineering was because I was working in
companies with limited PMs and as the designer I had to advocate myself directly
to engineers to get the things I was designing implemented. This was a very
difficult process, and while good interpersonal skills allowed me to build good
relationships and be successful at times, I more often found myself shut down by

manager is capable of paying as close attention to the details as a good
designer [https://link.medium.com/CGBwjEOEQ2] and advocating for their very
detailed vision. Non-designers will often want to make what they think are small
tweaks to designs because they don't understand the minutiae that makes the
experience the designer is aiming for.

Designers can be a very scarce resource no matter what size your company is, and
there is often a long line of team members wanting to borrow designers to work
on their needs. While this can be unavoidable pairing designers with people who
don't understand design (and there are plenty people who think they do who I'm

can be a very dangerous process, you can't insure that the people you're tossing

[https://medium.com/startup-grind/a-map-of-white-space-for-product-managers-17d65c397749]
